You are no longer asking for “good sources.” You are asking for a **canonical reading ladder that grounds your system in:** 1. **computability (formal objects, embeddings, constraints)** 2. **information extraction (multimodal signals)** 3. **representation (embeddings, structure)** 4. **optimization + learning (attribution, causal inference)** 5. **systems theory (state, control, feedback)** 6. **aesthetics + semiotics (identity, symbols, narrative)** Below is the **exact ladder**, ordered so each layer *enables the next*. This is not a list; it is a dependency chain. --- # I. FOUNDATIONAL MATHEMATICS + COMPUTABILITY *(You cannot formalize Natural Center without this layer)* ### Core Objective Define what it means for an object to be **computable, representable, and optimizable** ### Canonical Sources - **entity["book","Introduction to Algorithms","Cormen et al"]** → formal algorithm design, asymptotics, optimization primitives - **entity["book","Concrete Mathematics","Knuth"]** → discrete structures, recurrence, combinatorics - **entity["book","The Art of Computer Programming","Knuth"]** → canonical reference for algorithmic rigor - **entity["book","Category Theory for the Sciences","David I. Spivak"]** → objects, morphisms, composability (critical for your pipeline abstraction) - **entity["book","Computability and Logic","Boolos, Burgess, Jeffrey"]** → what it means for something to be computable vs symbolic --- ### What this layer gives you Formal grounding for statements like: ```text Natural Center = computable object Brand Embedding = vector space representation System = composition of functions over state ``` Without this, your system remains metaphorical. --- # II. INFORMATION THEORY + SIGNAL EXTRACTION *(Required for your “signal extraction” stages)* ### Canonical Sources - **entity["book","Elements of Information Theory","Thomas M. Cover"]** → entropy, compression, signal vs noise - **entity["scientific_concept","Shannon Entropy"]** → formal basis of “low-entropy core” (your Natural Center synthesis) - **entity["book","Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning","Christopher M. Bishop"]** → probabilistic modeling, clustering, latent variables --- ### What this layer gives you Justification for: ```text $PROC_SIGNAL_EXTRACTION $PROC_SEMANTIC_CLUSTER low-entropy center synthesis ``` This is the mathematical basis for why your pipeline works at all. --- # III. REPRESENTATION LEARNING (EMBEDDINGS) *(Directly maps to your Brand Embedding layer)* ### Canonical Sources - **entity["paper","Attention Is All You Need","Vaswani et al"]** → transformer architecture (foundation of embeddings) - **entity["paper","CLIP: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision","OpenAI"]** → joint vision-language embedding (exactly your multimodal space) - **entity["paper","BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers","Devlin et al"]** → semantic embedding of language - **entity["scientific_concept","Word Embedding"]** → vector representation of meaning - **entity["scientific_concept","Latent Space"]** → where your Natural Center actually lives computationally --- ### What this layer gives you Formal justification for: ```text BE = Φ(T, A, τ, N, S, Ω) cosine similarity → identity validation embedding space → constraint surface ``` This is the **bridge between symbolic Natural Center and computation** fileciteturn0file17 --- # IV. CAUSAL INFERENCE + ATTRIBUTION *(Critical for your “audience engine” and economic model)* ### Canonical Sources - **entity["book","Causal Inference: The Mixtape","Scott Cunningham"]** - **entity["book","The Book of Why","Judea Pearl"]** - **entity["scientific_concept","Do Calculus","causal inference"]** --- ### What this layer gives you Formalizes: ```text Lift_NC = E[Y | high alignment] - E[Y | low alignment] multi-touch attribution causal vs correlational performance ``` This is exactly the missing layer identified in your attribution system fileciteturn0file14 --- # VI. CONTROL SYSTEMS + DYNAMICAL SYSTEMS *(Your system is not static—it evolves)* ### Canonical Sources - **entity["book","Feedback Systems","Åström & Murray"]** - **entity["scientific_concept","Control Theory"]** - **entity["scientific_concept","Dynamical System"]** --- ### What this layer gives you Formal grounding for: ```text NC(t+1) = NC(t) + constrained_update(...) feedback loops stability vs drift ``` This is your **system evolution law** fileciteturn0file19 --- # VII. ## Assistant (artifact)

Make sure to include fileciteturn0file1 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. Yes. The operator layer should now be built as a **production research-and-deployment system**, not a mere posting tool. And your platform-evolution requirement is correct: the system must treat platform conditions as a **time-varying environment**, because the success function on YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, and LinkedIn is not stable across eras. Official sources already expose some of the observable surfaces you need to model: YouTube provides channel- and video-level analytics such as audience retention, impressions CTR, watch time, and new/casual/regular viewer segmentation; TikTok exposes posting workflows and developer constraints through its Content Posting APIs and guidelines; LinkedIn exposes organic posting primitives and some company-level engagement data via its marketing APIs. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) # $OPERATOR_GRADE_SYSTEM = "CRONUS_METABOLUS_OS" ```bash $MODE = "full-stack system (multi-service, production-grade)" $SYSTEM_NAME = "Cronus-Metabolus" $SYSTEM_CLASS = "Temporal Content Yield + Audience Formation Operating System" $PRIMARY_GOAL = "Convert premium assets into compounding audience, attribution, and platform-adaptive growth" $SECONDARY_GOAL = "Continuously research, detect, and adapt to platform-state changes over time" ``` # I. What this operator layer actually is This layer is the executable counterpart to the dissertation layer. The dissertation layer defines the invariants, causal claims, theoretical mappings, and research questions. The operator layer turns those into a running system that does six things at once: ingest assets, generate outputs, distribute them, measure results, run experiments, and update its understanding of both audience behavior and platform change. Formally: ```text Asset → Natural Center Extraction → Platform-Aware Generation → Scheduling + Distribution → Analytics + Attribution → Experimentation + Causal Analysis → Platform Change Detection → Model / Rule Updates → Repeat ``` # II. Production-grade service map The correct architecture is multi-service. Monolith first would be faster, but it will fail the research requirement, because research observability, platform adapters, experimentation, and identity enforcement need separate contracts. ```bash $SERVICES = { gateway_api, auth_identity, asset_ingestion, media_analysis, natural_center, generation_orchestrator, platform_intelligence, scheduling_engine, publishing_connectors, analytics_ingestion, attribution_engine, experimentation_engine, research_registry, reporting_dashboard, model_registry, event_bus, data_warehouse, object_storage, workflow_orchestrator, alerting_observability } ``` ## 1. `$SERVICE_GATEWAY_API` This is the system edge. It accepts user, client, asset, campaign, and experiment requests. It should expose stable contracts to UI, internal operators, and later partner integrations. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Entry point for all control-plane requests" $OUTPUTS = "campaign creation, asset registration, experiment submission, report fetch" ``` ## 2. `$SERVICE_ASSET_INGESTION` This service normalizes source material. It accepts hero films, render sets, stills, transcripts, briefing docs, and brand corpus. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "File intake, metadata extraction, transcode initiation, checksum, lineage" ``` ## 3. `$SERVICE_MEDIA_ANALYSIS` This is the decomposition engine in production form. It performs scene detection, keyframe extraction, transcript alignment, pacing analysis, energy-point detection, and content segmentation. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Turn one dense asset into analyzable fragments" ``` ## 4. `$SERVICE_NATURAL_CENTER` This service computes and stores the identity core. It should enforce symbolic, tonal, aesthetic, and thematic invariants across all outputs. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Compute, version, validate, and evolve the Natural Center object" ``` ## 5. `$SERVICE_GENERATION_ORCHESTRATOR` This service creates the outputs. It composes platform-specific assets, caption variants, CTA variants, sequencing plans, and campaign bundles. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Generate platform-native outputs constrained by identity and platform profile" ``` ## 6. `$SERVICE_PLATFORM_INTELLIGENCE` This is now mandatory. It models each platform as a time-dependent system. It should ingest official platform documentation changes, API changes, available analytics fields, posting constraints, and internal performance shifts. YouTube’s analytics surfaces already include audience retention, traffic and audience segments, while TikTok and LinkedIn expose developer posting capabilities and versioning or access constraints through official docs; those should be treated as structured inputs to this service. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Track platform state, detect change, update generation and scheduling policy" ``` ## 7. `$SERVICE_SCHEDULING_ENGINE` This service determines when each output should be published, republished, remixed, or retired. It consumes audience timing, asset decay models, and platform profile data. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Temporal orchestration" ``` ## 8. `$SERVICE_PUBLISHING_CONNECTORS` This is the execution layer. It handles the actual post creation and retrieval integrations. TikTok’s Content Posting APIs support direct posting or draft upload flows; LinkedIn’s Posts API supports creation and retrieval of organic and sponsored posts, with content-type constraints and versioned API requirements. ([developers.tiktok.com](https://developers.tiktok.com/products/content-posting-api?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Platform-specific publish / fetch adapters" ``` ## 9. `$SERVICE_ANALYTICS_INGESTION` This service pulls analytics and normalizes them into a common schema. YouTube official analytics surfaces support view, engagement, retention, CTR, and audience segmentation at varying levels, though some data may be limited depending on thresholds and filters. That limitation itself must be modeled as a reliability constraint. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Collect raw performance telemetry and normalize it" ``` ## 10. `$SERVICE_ATTRIBUTION_ENGINE` This is where the product stops being “content software” and becomes an economic system. It traces every post, fragment, caption, CTA, campaign, and asset back to origin. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Asset-level and fragment-level yield accounting" ``` ## 11. `$SERVICE_EXPERIMENTATION_ENGINE` This service owns A/B tests, bandits, holdouts, control/treatment assignment, confidence intervals, and causal inference workflow. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Statistically disciplined optimization" ``` ## 12. `$SERVICE_RESEARCH_REGISTRY` This is the bridge between dissertation-grade and operator-grade. Every hypothesis, paper mapping, experiment, metric definition, and platform-state memo should live here. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Research memory and epistemic governance" ``` ## 13. `$SERVICE_REPORTING_DASHBOARD` This serves operators, clients, and future investors. It must expose asset yield, audience movement, platform differences, and trend drift. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Human-readable operational truth" ``` # III. Canonical data domains You need four data planes, not one. ```bash $DATA_PLANES = { source_plane, operational_plane, experimental_plane, evolutionary_plane } ``` ## `$SOURCE_PLANE` This contains hero assets, renders, transcripts, briefs, brand corpus, prior posts, style references. ## `$OPERATIONAL_PLANE` This contains generated posts, publish events, platform IDs, schedules, revisions, connector logs. ## `$EXPERIMENTAL_PLANE` This contains hypotheses, treatments, controls, randomization groups, test windows, significance results. ## `$EVOLUTIONARY_PLANE` This contains platform snapshots, platform doc changes, field additions/removals, policy updates, performance regime shifts, and internal adaptation rules. # IV. Core database objects The system should be object-oriented at the ontology level and event-driven at the execution level. ```yaml Asset: asset_id: string source_uri: string asset_type: video|image_set|render_set|document brand_id: string checksum: string created_at: datetime Fragment: fragment_id: string asset_id: string fragment_type: clip|frame|crop|layout start_ms: int end_ms: int energy_score: float semantic_cluster: string NaturalCenter: nc_id: string brand_id: string thematic_core: json tonal_vector: json aesthetic_signature: json symbolic_markers: json invariants: json mutation_bounds: json version: int PlatformProfile: profile_id: string platform: youtube|instagram|tiktok|linkedin effective_from: date effective_to: date|null format_weights: json known_constraints: json ranking_assumptions: json confidence_score: float GeneratedUnit: unit_id: string fragment_id: string platform: string unit_type: short_video|carousel|image_post|text_post caption_variant_id: string cta_variant_id: string nc_version: int platform_profile_id: string PublishEvent: publish_id: string unit_id: string platform: string external_post_id: string published_at: datetime schedule_policy_id: string PerformanceObservation: obs_id: string publish_id: string observed_at: datetime metrics: json source_reliability: float Experiment: experiment_id: string hypothesis_id: string treatment_policy: json control_policy: json start_at: datetime end_at: datetime status: draft|running|complete PlatformChangeEvent: change_id: string platform: string detected_at: datetime change_type: api|analytics_field|format|policy|behavioral_shift evidence_uri: string effect_hypothesis: string severity: low|medium|high ``` # V. Platform evolution as a first-class subsystem This is the part most teams omit. It should not be “notes in a document.” It should be a live intelligence loop. ```bash $SUBSYSTEM_PLATFORM_EVOLUTION = { documentation_monitor, analytics_schema_monitor, posting_capability_monitor, performance_regime_monitor, historical_phase_model } ``` ## `$DOCUMENTATION_MONITOR` This tracks official docs, release notes, and API version changes. LinkedIn’s official docs already show versioned API behavior and deprecation notices, which is exactly the type of change this monitor must capture. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/linkedin/marketing/community-management/shares/posts-api?view=li-lms-2026-01&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$ANALYTICS_SCHEMA_MONITOR` This tracks whether a platform adds, removes, or changes analytics surfaces. On YouTube, limited-data conditions must be tracked because they alter what inference is valid. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$POSTING_CAPABILITY_MONITOR` This tracks what formats and workflows are currently supported. TikTok’s direct-post versus draft-upload modes and LinkedIn’s content-type support are examples. ([developers.tiktok.com](https://developers.tiktok.com/products/content-posting-api?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$PERFORMANCE_REGIME_MONITOR` This is internal. It detects when yesterday’s winning structures stop winning. Example: same asset class, same audience band, same cadence, but retention or follow-conversion drops across a time window. ## `$HISTORICAL_PHASE_MODEL` This stores platform eras. That lets you encode your point directly: ```yaml PlatformEra: platform: youtube era_id: YT_2010_CREATOR_GRAPH start_date: 2008-01-01 end_date: 2012-12-31 dominant_mechanism: "subscriber/personality pull" success_features: ["personality continuity", "episode loyalty", "creator identity"] ``` Then later: ```yaml PlatformEra: platform: youtube era_id: YT_2020_RETENTION_RECOMMENDER start_date: 2019-01-01 end_date: null dominant_mechanism: "recommendation + retention + multi-format coexistence" success_features: ["hook quality", "retention curve", "packaging", "viewer segmentation"] ``` The exact dates should remain provisional research objects unless directly established by your source methodology. The point is structural: **platform-state(t)** must exist as a model variable. # VI. The research operating system inside production The system must preserve dissertation-grade rigor while running. ```bash $RESEARCH_OS = { hypothesis_registry, source_registry, experiment_registry, evidence_store, conclusion_registry } ``` ## `$HYPOTHESIS_REGISTRY` Every important claim gets an ID. ```yaml Hypothesis: hypothesis_id: HYP_000127 title: "Open-loop short-form sequencing increases return-viewer ratio on platform X" pillar: behavioral_response platform_scope: ["youtube","tiktok"] temporal_scope: "current" status: proposed|running|supported|rejected ``` ## `$SOURCE_REGISTRY` This stores both secondary and primary sources. Secondary sources support theory. Primary sources support market and platform standing. For platform standing, official platform docs and analytics documentation qualify as primary sources for platform capabilities and measurement surfaces. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$EXPERIMENT_REGISTRY` This records every test and its outcome. ## `$EVIDENCE_STORE` This stores screenshots, exports, API payloads, retention curves, docs snapshots, and internal memos. ## `$CONCLUSION_REGISTRY` This contains accepted operating rules, each linked to evidence. # VII. Experiment system design Your production system should treat every campaign as an experimentable object. ```bash $EXPERIMENT_TYPES = { hook_test, caption_test, cta_test, sequencing_test, schedule_test, format_test, identity_coherence_test, platform_policy_test, repost_decay_test } ``` Example: ```yaml HookTest: treatment: "0-2 second hard visual interruption" control: "0-2 second context lead-in" primary_metric: "3s retention" secondary_metric: "follow conversion" stratification: ["platform","audience_band","asset_class"] ``` # VIII. Production metrics hierarchy Do not flatten everything into “engagement.” ```bash $METRIC_HIERARCHY = { attention_metrics, identity_metrics, audience_metrics, economic_metrics, platform_evolution_metrics } ``` ## `$ATTENTION_METRICS` On platforms where available, these should include retention-related measures, watch-time structures, and packaging effects. YouTube explicitly surfaces audience retention, average view duration, impressions CTR, and audience categories such as new, casual, and regular viewers. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$IDENTITY_METRICS` These are internal, not native platform metrics. ```bash $IDENTITY_METRICS = { natural_center_similarity, cross-post_symbolic_consistency, tonal_drift_score, aesthetic_signature_deviation } ``` ## `$AUDIENCE_METRICS` ```bash $AUDIENCE_METRICS = { new_viewer_rate, returning_viewer_rate, follower_conversion_rate, repeat_engagement_rate, lead_conversion_rate } ``` ## `$ECONOMIC_METRICS` ```bash $ECONOMIC_METRICS = { asset_yield_multiplier, cost_per_generated_unit, cost_per_follower, cost_per_lead, gross_asset_return } ``` ## `$PLATFORM_EVOLUTION_METRICS` ```bash $PLATFORM_EVOLUTION_METRICS = { strategy_decay_rate, platform_shift_alert_count, ranking_assumption_confidence, connector_capability_delta } ``` # IX. Workflow orchestration Use an event bus plus a workflow engine. ```bash $ARCHITECTURE_STYLE = "event-driven control plane + async workflow execution" ``` A minimal event vocabulary: ```text asset.registered asset.transcoded fragments.generated natural_center.computed platform_profile.updated units.generated schedule.computed publish.completed analytics.ingested experiment.started experiment.completed platform_change.detected policy.updated ``` # X. Infrastructure pattern Use containerized microservices, object storage, warehouse, queue, and workflow orchestrator. ```bash $INFRA = { compute: "Kubernetes or equivalent container orchestration", storage: "object store for media + warehouse for analytics", queue: "Kafka or equivalent event bus", workflow: "Temporal or equivalent durable workflow engine", cache: "Redis or equivalent", db: "Postgres for operational truth", observability: "OpenTelemetry + logs + traces + alerting" } ``` # XI. Environment-variable contract Per your standing preference, the system should be parameterized from the start. ```bash $APP_ENV $SYSTEM_ID $PUBLIC_APP_URI $GATEWAY_PORT $POSTGRES_URI $REDIS_URI $EVENT_BUS_URI $WORKFLOW_ENGINE_URI $OBJECT_STORE_URI $DATA_WAREHOUSE_URI $MODEL_REGISTRY_URI $EVIDENCE_STORE_URI $OPENAI_API_KEY $EMBEDDING_MODEL_ID $GENERATION_MODEL_ID $VISION_MODEL_ID $YOUTUBE_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $TIKTOK_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $LINKEDIN_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $INSTAGRAM_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $YOUTUBE_CLIENT_ID $YOUTUBE_CLIENT_SECRET $TIKTOK_CLIENT_ID $TIKTOK_CLIENT_SECRET $LINKEDIN_CLIENT_ID $LINKEDIN_CLIENT_SECRET $NATURAL_CENTER_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD $TONAL_DRIFT_THRESHOLD $EXPERIMENT_MIN_SAMPLE_SIZE $SIGNIFICANCE_ALPHA $PLATFORM_SHIFT_ALERT_THRESHOLD $ASSET_RETIREMENT_HALF_LIFE_DAYS ``` # XII. Deployment topology There are three correct environments. ```bash $DEPLOYMENT_STAGES = { research, staging, production } ``` ## `$RESEARCH` Loose, hypothesis-heavy, lower automation, high logging. ## `$STAGING` Connector-safe, sandbox posting where possible, simulated analytics replay. ## `$PRODUCTION` Version-locked connectors, auditable experiments, strict approval policies. # XIII. Human roles in the loop Production-grade does not mean human-free. ```bash $ROLES = { research_lead, platform_analyst, experiment_owner, brand_identity_editor, campaign_operator, data_engineer, ml_engineer, account_director } ``` The key distinction is this: humans set constraints, approve escalations, and interpret anomalies. The system does the repetition. # XIV. Failure handling A real system needs explicit failure classes. ```bash $FAILURE_CLASSES = { connector_failure, analytics_gap, identity_drift, platform_policy_conflict, experiment_invalidity, low-signal asset, attribution_break, strategy_decay } ``` Example responses: ```yaml StrategyDecay: trigger: "rolling 21-day drop in core KPI across matched cohorts" response: "freeze auto-promotion of winning strategy, create platform-shift investigation" ``` # XV. The first production build order This should be phased. Not because the rest is unimportant, but because this is the shortest route to a functioning research-grade machine. ## Phase 1 Build `asset_ingestion`, `media_analysis`, `natural_center`, `generation_orchestrator`, `publishing_connectors`, `analytics_ingestion`, `reporting_dashboard`. ## Phase 2 Add `attribution_engine`, `experimentation_engine`, `research_registry`. ## Phase 3 Add `platform_intelligence`, `platform_change_detection`, `policy auto-update`. ## Phase 4 Add cross-client learning, adaptive scheduling, and semi-autonomous campaign policies. # XVI. The repo skeleton ```bash $REPO_ROOT/ apps/ gateway-api/ operator-console/ reporting-dashboard/ services/ auth-identity/ asset-ingestion/ media-analysis/ natural-center/ generation-orchestrator/ platform-intelligence/ scheduling-engine/ publishing-connectors/ analytics-ingestion/ attribution-engine/ experimentation-engine/ research-registry/ model-registry/ alerting-observability/ packages/ domain-models/ event-schemas/ metric-definitions/ platform-adapters/ natural-center-sdk/ experiment-sdk/ analytics-normalizers/ shared-config/ infra/ terraform/ kubernetes/ workflows/ monitoring/ data/ db-migrations/ warehouse-models/ platform-era-seeds/ research/ sources/ hypotheses/ memos/ reports/ platform-change-log/ docs/ architecture/ runbooks/ contracts/ api/ ``` # XVII. Final operating doctrine The production system should obey four non-negotiable laws. ```text Law 1: Every output must inherit from a Natural Center. Law 2: Every campaign must be attributable back to source assets. Law 3: Every optimization must be experimentally legible. Law 4: Every platform assumption must be time-bounded and revisable. ``` That fourth law is the direct answer to your YouTube point. What made a YouTube star in 2010 should not be treated as “wrong”; it should be treated as a different solution under a different platform state. The system therefore needs historical phase memory, current-state sensing, and future-state adaptation, rather than one timeless content doctrine. # XVIII. What logically comes next The next artifact should be the build sheet that turns this into execution. ```bash $NEXT_ARTIFACT = "FULL_STACK_IMPLEMENTATION_BLUEPRINT" ``` That blueprint should include service contracts, event schemas, database migrations, workflow definitions, metric formulas, and the first 90-day build plan.

PILLAR 4 — BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS ## Canonical Sources ```text - Thaler & Sunstein — Nudge (2008) - Cialdini — Influence (1984) - Ariely — Predictably Irrational (2008) ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - heuristics - bias - social proof - commitment loops ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Human irrationality modeled ### Present Gap Content ≠ behavior change ### Future ```text content as state-transition trigger ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "CTA + Conversion Engine" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text Which triggers move viewer → follower? ``` --- # VI. PILLAR 6 — PLATFORM ECONOMICS ## Canonical Sources ```text - Rochet & Tirole — Two-Sided Markets - Parker, Van Alstyne — Platform Revolution - Varian — Economics of Information ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - network effects - supply/demand balancing - platform incentives ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Market structures analyzed ### Present Gap Creators do not model platform incentives ### Future ```text content aligned to platform economic incentives ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "Platform Intelligence" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text What content features platforms reward? ``` --- # VIII. RECOMMENDED FINAL FORM ```bash $SYSTEM_NAME = "Cronus-Metabolus" $SYSTEM_ROLE = "Temporal Content Yield Engine" ``` This preserves your symbolic structure while aligning with: - time - transformation - measurable output --- If you proceed, next logical step is: **formalizing the internal ontology layer** so that: - Cronus = temporal controller - Metabolus = transformation pipeline I can define that as a strict module architecture next.

Make sure to include fileciteturn0file11 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. The figure you’re referring to is **entity["mythological_figure","Cronus","Greek Titan who devoured his children"]** (Greek mythology), known in Roman mythology as **entity["mythological_figure","Saturn","Roman counterpart of Cronus"]**. ### Core Myth (precise form) Cronus, a Titan and ruler of the cosmos before the Olympians, was told by prophecy that one of his children would overthrow him. To prevent this, he **swallowed each child at birth**. ### Children involved The children he consumed were: - Hestia - Demeter - Hera - Hades - Poseidon His wife, **Rhea**, eventually intervened: - She hid their youngest child, **Zeus** - She gave Cronus a **stone wrapped in cloth**, which he swallowed instead ### Resolution When Zeus matured, he forced Cronus to **regurgitate his siblings**, and then led the Olympians in overthrowing him (the Titanomachy). ### Structural interpretation (useful for your system work) Cronus represents: - **Self-consuming systems** (systems that destroy their own outputs to preserve control) - **Fear-driven recursion failure** - **Temporal devouring** (time consuming its own productions — later philosophically mapped onto Saturn/Time) If you want, I can map Cronus into your myth→technology assignment framework (e.g., which system archetype or computational failure mode he encodes).

`policy-engine/`, `evidence-store-indexer/`, and environment-scoped infrastructure roots. The source materials already require the service map, research registry, platform-evolution subsystem, and repo skeleton; this is the hardened version. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## What each top-level directory is for `apps/` is the control plane. It is where humans touch the system. `services/` is the execution plane. Every major invariant-bearing capability gets its own service because the source explicitly rejects a shallow posting tool and pushes toward a research-and-deployment system with separate contracts for identity, experimentation, and platform adaptation. fileciteturn2file2 `packages/` is the semantic core. It prevents drift between services by centralizing domain objects, event schemas, metrics, adapter interfaces, and shared validation. `infra/` is the runtime substrate. The source set explicitly calls for container orchestration, object storage, warehouse, event bus, durable workflow engine, cache, and observability. fileciteturn2file2 `data/` is schema truth plus repeatable seeds. It holds migrations, warehouse models, and platform-era seeds for `platform_state(t)`. `research/` is not optional. The source set treats hypotheses, evidence, experiment definitions, and platform change as first-class production concerns, not side documents. fileciteturn2file2 `clients/` is where brand-specific Natural Center projections, campaign policies, and deployment constraints live. This is how the system remains multi-tenant without dissolving identity. `contracts/` exists so API, events, database schema, metrics, and experiment objects can be versioned independently and validated in CI. ## Service decomposition The correct first-wave service boundary is this: ```text gateway-api auth-identity asset-ingestion media-analysis natural-center generation-orchestrator publishing-connectors analytics-ingestion reporting-dashboard ``` That is directly aligned with the build phases and MVP definition in the implementation blueprint. fileciteturn2file1 The second wave is: ```text attribution-engine experimentation-engine research-registry platform-intelligence policy-engine ``` That matches the transition from “content multiplier” to “audience formation + research-integrated system.” fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Internal service file template Use the same internal file tree for every service. ```text services/<service-name>/ ├── src/ │ ├── application/ │ ├── domain/ │ ├── infrastructure/ │ ├── interfaces/ │ ├── workers/ │ └── index.ts ├── contracts/ ├── tests/ │ ├── unit/ │ ├── integration/ │ └── contract/ ├── Dockerfile ├── package.json ├── tsconfig.json └── README.md ``` This gives you stable separation between domain logic, ports/adapters, worker processes, and external contracts. ## Canonical domain objects These should live in `packages/domain-models/` and be imported everywhere else: ```text Asset Fragment NaturalCenter GeneratedUnit PublishEvent PerformanceObservation Experiment PlatformProfile PlatformChangeEvent Campaign BrandProfile CaptionVariant CTAVariant SchedulePolicy EvidenceRecord Hypothesis Conclusion ``` The first eight are explicitly present in the source blueprint. The remaining ones are necessary completion objects once you operationalize campaigns, CTA testing, research evidence, and client configuration. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Event vocabulary Put these in `packages/event-schemas/`. ```text asset.registered asset.transcoded fragments.generated natural_center.computed natural_center.validated platform_profile.updated units.generated schedule.computed publish.requested publish.completed analytics.ingested attribution.computed experiment.started experiment.completed platform_change.detected policy.updated identity_drift.detected strategy_decay.detected ``` The first set is directly present in the blueprint. The drift and decay events are required by the failure-handling and platform-adaptation logic. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Full environment variable contract Below is the environment contract I would actually ship. ---------------------------------------- # ANALYTICS / ATTRIBUTION # ---------------------------------------- $ANALYTICS_POLL_CRON=0 */6 * * * $ANALYTICS_BACKFILL_DAYS=30 $MIN_ANALYTICS_RELIABILITY_SCORE=0.70 $ATTRIBUTION_LOOKBACK_DAYS=90 $ASSET_RETIREMENT_HALF_LIFE_DAYS=21 $FOLLOW_CONVERSION_LOOKBACK_DAYS=30 $REPORT_DEFAULT_WINDOW_DAYS=30 # ---------------------------------------- # EXPERIMENTATION / STATISTICS # ---------------------------------------- $EXPERIMENT_MIN_SAMPLE_SIZE=1000 $SIGNIFICANCE_ALPHA=0.05 $EXPERIMENT_DEFAULT_POWER=0.80 $EXPERIMENT_HOLDOUT_RATIO=0.10 $ALLOW_MULTI_ARMED_BANDITS=false $DEFAULT_PRIMARY_METRIC=retention_3s $DEFAULT_SECONDARY_METRIC=follower_conversion_rate # ---------------------------------------- # SCHEDULING / DEPLOYMENT # ---------------------------------------- $SCHEDULING_ENABLED=true $DEFAULT_SCHEDULING_POLICY=engagement_weighted $MAX_POSTS_PER_DAY_PER_PLATFORM=3 $MIN_HOURS_BETWEEN_POSTS=4 $RETRY_CONNECTOR_ATTEMPTS=3 $PUBLISH_DRY_RUN=false $APPROVAL_REQUIRED_FOR_PUBLISH=true # ---------------------------------------- # CLIENT / TENANCY # ---------------------------------------- $DEFAULT_BRAND_ID= $DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID= $MULTI_TENANT_MODE=true $CLIENT_CONFIG_STRICT_VALIDATION=true $WHITE_LABEL_MODE=false # ---------------------------------------- # OBSERVABILITY # ---------------------------------------- $OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT= $METRICS_EXPORT_ENABLED=true $TRACING_ENABLED=true $ERROR_REPORTING_DSN= $ALERT_EMAIL_FROM= $ALERT_EMAIL_TO= $SLACK_WEBHOOK_URI= # ---------------------------------------- # CI / TEST / LOCAL DEV # ---------------------------------------- $CI=false $TEST_POSTGRES_URI= $TEST_REDIS_URI= $TEST_OBJECT_STORE_URI= $ENABLE_REPLAY_TESTS=true $ENABLE_LOAD_TESTS=false $SEED_PLATFORM_ERAS=true ``` ## `.env` file layout Use four environment templates. ```text .env.example .env.research.example .env.staging.example .env.production.example ``` `research` should allow looser policies, high logging, replay mode, and partial connector disablement. `staging` should use sandbox credentials, dry-run publishing, and analytics replay. `production` should lock connectors, require approvals where configured, and enforce drift thresholds. Those three environments are explicitly required in the source material. fileciteturn2file2 ## Package manager and language choice The source set does not prescribe language. The cleanest fit is: ```bash $MONOREPO_TOOL="pnpm + turbo" $PRIMARY_LANGUAGE="typescript" $WORKFLOW_RUNTIME="temporal" $DB="postgres" $CACHE="redis" $QUEUE="kafka-or-equivalent" ``` That matches the service-heavy, contract-heavy, event-driven structure in the documents. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Suggested top-level governance files ```text README.md REPO_GOVERNANCE.md ARCHITECTURE.md ONTOLOGY.md ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLES.md EVENT_CATALOG.md METRIC_CATALOG.md EXPERIMENT_POLICY.md PLATFORM_EVOLUTION_POLICY.md NATURAL_CENTER_POLICY.md ``` These matter because the repo is doing double duty as execution system and epistemic system. ## Minimal first implementation slice If the goal is the shortest path to a real first build, this is the initial thin slice: ```text apps/gateway-api apps/operator-console apps/reporting-dashboard services/asset-ingestion services/media-analysis services/natural-center services/generation-orchestrator services/publishing-connectors services/analytics-ingestion packages/domain-models packages/event-schemas packages/metric-definitions packages/natural-center-sdk packages/shared-config infra/workflows/temporal data/db-migrations/postgres clients/templates contracts/api contracts/events ``` That is the strict MVP implied by the original product document and the later implementation blueprint: upload asset, extract fragments, generate posts, publish, and report. fileciteturn2file0turn2file1 ## Bottom-line translation Your sources resolve into this architecture: ```text market claim: one premium asset -> many platform-native outputs + measurable ROI operator reality: source plane -> natural center -> platform-aware generation -> scheduling -> publishing -> analytics -> attribution -> experimentation -> adaptation ``` That is the correct repository frame. fileciteturn2file0turn2file1turn2file2 The next logical artifact is a full `README.md + .env.example + monorepo bootstrap scaffold` in code form.

## Assistant (artifact)

Make sure to include fileciteturn0file13 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. I reviewed the source set. The core requirements are consistent across the documents: one premium asset must be decomposed into fragments, constrained by a computable Natural Center, adapted by platform profile, published, then scored back to asset-, fragment-, and audience-level outcomes. The source corpus explicitly defines the intended flow as asset ingestion, media analysis, Natural Center extraction, generation, publishing, analytics ingestion, attribution, experimentation, and platform adaptation. fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file1 fileciteturn2file2 ## Exact algorithmic frame The cleanest formalization is this: ```text content = f(asset, natural_center, platform_profile) success = f(content, platform_state(t), audience_state) ``` That is already implicit in your sources: the system is not a scheduler; it is an extraction-and-yield engine built around premium source material, Natural Center enforcement, and time-varying platform conditions. fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file1 I would define the system as seven strict algorithms: ```text ALG_01 = multimodal asset normalization ALG_02 = multimodal fragment extraction ALG_03 = natural center derivation ALG_04 = fragment scoring ALG_05 = platform-fit scoring ALG_06 = audience-transition scoring ALG_07 = post-publication attribution and learning ``` --- ## 1. ALG_01 — multimodal asset normalization Purpose: convert heterogeneous input into a unified analyzable representation. ### Accepted input classes Your sources already imply these classes: ```text video image_set / render_set audio text / brand voice corpus historical post corpus ``` fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file2 ### Canonical normalized object ```json { "asset_id": "A_*", "asset_type": "video|image_set|audio|document|mixed", "streams": { "video": [...], "audio": [...], "text": [...], "image": [...] }, "metadata": { "duration_ms": 0, "fps": 0, "resolution": [0, 0], "language": "en", "brand_id": "B_*" } } ``` ### Exact procedure ```text PROC_NORMALIZE(asset): 1. Compute checksum(asset) 2. Detect asset_type 3. If video: demux into video stream + audio stream transcode to canonical formats sample frames at adaptive interval 4. If image_set/render_set: normalize dimensions, color profile, EXIF 5. If audio: normalize sample rate and loudness 6. If text: clean, segment, and encode paragraphs/sentences 7. Persist all derivative streams 8. Emit asset.normalized ``` --- ## 2. ALG_02 — multimodal fragment extraction Purpose: produce candidate units from dense assets. Your sources already call for clip extraction at high-energy moments, scene detection, keyframe extraction, pacing analysis, and segmentation. fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file1 ### 2.1 Video fragment extraction For video, define fragments over a change-point lattice rather than fixed windows. ```text Let V = video timeline Let C = set of candidate cut points C = union( scene boundaries, shot boundaries, transcript punctuation peaks, silence boundaries, audio onset peaks, motion regime changes ) ``` Construct candidate intervals: ```text F_video = {(c_i, c_j) | min_len <= c_j - c_i <= max_len} ``` Recommended bounds: ```text min_len = 3s max_len = 45s platform-specific downstream pruning applies ``` ### 2.2 Image fragment extraction For renders and stills, fragments are not temporal; they are compositional derivatives. ```text F_image = union( full-frame, crop_subject, crop_detail, before_after_pair, comparison_layout, lifestyle_context_projection, carousel_sequence ) ``` ### 2.3 Audio fragment extraction ```text F_audio = union( speech segment, hook phrase, emphasis burst, music drop, silence-contrast interval ) ``` ### 2.4 Text fragment extraction ```text F_text = union( claim, hook line, authority line, transformation line, CTA line, FAQ line ) ``` ### 2.5 Exact multimodal candidate generation ```text PROC_EXTRACT_FRAGMENTS(asset): 1. Detect scene boundaries on video 2. Detect shot boundaries 3. ASR transcript with token timestamps 4. Compute audio envelope, spectral flux, silence regions 5. Compute motion magnitude and saliency over time 6. Merge all boundary proposals 7. Generate candidate windows 8. Generate image crops/layout variants 9. Generate text units from transcript and corpus 10. Return multimodal candidate set F ``` --- ## 3. ALG_03 — Natural Center derivation This is already partially specified in the source corpus: thematic core, aesthetic signature, tonal vector, narrative bias, symbolic markers, negative space, invariants, mutation bounds. fileciteturn2file2 ### Exact Natural Center object ```json { "nc_id": "NC_*", "thematic_core": [t1, t2, t3, t4, t5], "aesthetic_signature": { "palette": [...], "motion": [...], "composition": [...], "texture": [...] }, "tonal_vector": [formal, expressive, emotional, authoritative, playful], "narrative_bias": [transformation, tension, reveal, aspiration, proof], "symbolic_markers": [...], "negative_space": [...], "invariants": [...], "mutation_bounds": {...}, "embedding": [...] } ``` ### Exact derivation algorithm ```text PROC_DERIVE_NATURAL_CENTER(corpus): 1. For all visual assets: extract color histograms, embeddings, composition maps, motion stats 2. For all text/audio assets: extract sentence-length distribution, lexical density, rhetoric markers, semantic embeddings 3. Cluster semantic embeddings -> candidate themes 4. Select stable top-k themes across corpus 5. Compute aesthetic centroid across visual features 6. Compute tonal centroid across language features 7. Detect recurring symbolic elements 8. Detect systematic absences -> negative_space 9. Generate invariants from high-frequency stable features 10. Set mutation bounds from historical variance 11. Encode final Natural Center embedding ``` ### Stability rule A feature enters the Natural Center only if: ```text stability(feature) = presence_across_assets * consistency_across_assets accept iff stability(feature) >= τ_stability ``` --- ## 4. ALG_04 — fragment scoring This is the core extraction-scoring layer you asked for. Each candidate fragment gets a multimodal score vector, then a scalar aggregate. ### 4.1 Base feature vector per fragment For each fragment `f`, compute: ```text x(f) = [ visual_saliency, motion_energy, cut_density, subject_prominence, face_presence, product_presence, text_overlay_legibility, audio_energy, speech_clarity, hook_density, semantic_novelty, thematic_alignment, tonal_alignment, narrative_tension, symbolic_marker_density, platform_shape_fit ] ``` ### 4.2 Exact sub-scores #### Visual saliency ```text visual_saliency(f) = mean_t saliency_map_intensity(t) over fragment ``` #### Motion energy ```text motion_energy(f) = mean_t ||optical_flow(t)|| normalized by asset baseline ``` #### Cut density ```text cut_density(f) = number_of_shot_changes / fragment_duration_sec ``` #### Subject prominence ```text subject_prominence(f) = mean_t (largest_subject_bbox_area / frame_area) ``` #### Product presence ```text product_presence(f) = visible_time_of_primary_object / fragment_duration ``` #### Audio energy ```text audio_energy(f) = RMS(audio_signal_f) z-scored within asset ``` #### Speech clarity ```text speech_clarity(f) = 1 - WER_proxy(f) ``` Use ASR confidence as a proxy if needed. #### Hook density Define hooks as high-information lines or events in the first N seconds. ```text hook_density(f) = (count of hook tokens + count of high-saliency events in first 3s) / 3 ``` #### Semantic novelty ```text semantic_novelty(f) = distance(embedding(f), centroid(previous_selected_fragments)) ``` This prevents near-duplicate output. #### Thematic alignment ```text thematic_alignment(f) = max cosine_similarity(embedding(f), thematic_core_i) ``` #### Tonal alignment ```text tonal_alignment(f) = 1 - normalized_distance(tone_vector(f), tone_vector(NC)) ``` #### Narrative tension ```text narrative_tension(f) = weighted_sum( unresolved_question_presence, contrast_event_density, reveal_probability, expectation_violation ) ``` #### Symbolic marker density ```text symbolic_marker_density(f) = matched_symbolic_markers / possible_symbolic_markers ``` #### Platform shape fit This is structural, before any performance data. ```text platform_shape_fit(f,p) = g(duration_fit, aspect_ratio_fit, opening_velocity_fit, subtitle_density_fit) ``` ### 4.3 Hard constraint gating Before aggregate scoring, fail fragments that violate invariant rules: ```text if violates_negative_space(f, NC): reject if tonal_alignment(f) < τ_tone: reject if text_legibility(f,p) < τ_legibility: reject if safe_crop_confidence(f,p) < τ_crop: reject ``` ### 4.4 Aggregate fragment score ```text fragment_score(f,p) = w1*visual_saliency + w2*motion_energy + w3*subject_prominence + w4*audio_energy + w5*hook_density + w6*thematic_alignment + w7*tonal_alignment + w8*narrative_tension + w9*symbolic_marker_density + w10*semantic_novelty + w11*platform_shape_fit - w12*redundancy_penalty - w13*constraint_risk ``` Where: ```text redundancy_penalty = max similarity to already-selected fragments constraint_risk = predicted probability of violating NC or platform constraints ``` ### 4.5 Selection rule ```text select top-N fragments per platform subject to: pairwise similarity <= τ_dup thematic coverage >= τ_coverage duration bucket diversity narrative role diversity ``` This is better than naive top-N. --- ## 5. ALG_05 — platform-fit scoring Your sources explicitly require platform-aware generation and platform profiles with ranking features, format weights, timing bias, and time-varying platform state. fileciteturn2file1 ### Platform profile ```json { "platform": "tiktok", "format_weights": { "short_video": 0.8, "carousel": 0.1, "text": 0.1 }, "ranking_features": ["hook_retention", "replayability", "comment_rate"], "timing_bias": {...}, "constraints": {...}, "confidence_score": 0.82 } ``` ### Exact platform-fit score ```text platform_fit(f,p,t) = a1*duration_fit(f,p) + a2*opening_pattern_fit(f,p) + a3*caption_density_fit(f,p) + a4*loopability(f,p) + a5*replayability(f,p) + a6*format_weight_match(f,p,t) + a7*current_platform_preference_match(p,t) ``` ### Platform-specific heuristics ```text YouTube Shorts: emphasize clarity + retention + pay-off density TikTok: emphasize opening disruption + replayability + comment provocation LinkedIn: emphasize authority framing + slower pacing + insight density ``` That direction is consistent with the source set’s platform-profile logic. fileciteturn2file1 --- ## 6. ALG_06 — audience-transition scoring Your documents already move beyond engagement into audience-state transitions: viewer, engaged viewer, follower, returning follower, converted user, advocate. fileciteturn1file6 ### State machine ```text S0 unaware S1 viewer S2 engaged_viewer S3 follower S4 returning_follower S5 converted_user S6 advocate ``` ### Transition probabilities For each published unit `u`: ```text P(S2|S1,u) = sigmoid(β0 + β1*retention_3s + β2*completion_rate + β3*symbolic_marker_density + β4*hook_density) P(S3|S2,u) = sigmoid(γ0 + γ1*identity_binding + γ2*cta_strength + γ3*authority_signal + γ4*repeat_exposure_count) P(S4|S3,u) = sigmoid(δ0 + δ1*seriality + δ2*cadence_match + δ3*theme_continuity) P(S5|S4,u) = sigmoid(η0 + η1*offer_relevance + η2*trust_signal + η3*proof_density) ``` ### Identity binding score Since the sources insist that Natural Center persistence matters, define: ```text identity_binding(u) = b1*tonal_alignment + b2*symbolic_marker_density + b3*aesthetic_signature_match + b4*theme_continuity ``` This supports the “consistency -> recognition -> retention -> conversion” claim already present in the corpus. fileciteturn1file7 --- ## 7. ALG_07 — attribution and learning The sources already specify the required mapping: ```text performance -> publish_event -> unit -> fragment -> asset ``` and continuous experimentation with significance testing. fileciteturn2file1 ### Exact attribution chain ```text PROC_ATTRIBUTE(obs): 1. Resolve publish_id from platform event 2. Resolve unit_id from publish_id 3. Resolve fragment_id from unit_id 4. Resolve asset_id from fragment_id 5. Write observation to all four levels: unit fragment asset natural_center_version ``` ### Learning update ```text For each feature k: weight_k(t+1) = weight_k(t) + λ * contribution_k_to_success - μ * contribution_k_to_failure ``` Where success is not raw engagement only; it is a weighted portfolio: ```text success_score(u) = c1*attention_efficiency + c2*follow_conversion + c3*retention_ratio + c4*lead_conversion + c5*content_half_life ``` The source set already defines these downstream metrics directionally. fileciteturn1file6turn2file1 --- ## 8. Exact scoring stack This is the full stack I would use. ```text score_total(u,p,t) = α1 * score_fragment + α2 * score_natural_center_match + α3 * score_platform_fit + α4 * score_predicted_audience_transition + α5 * score_diversity_contribution + α6 * score_economic_yield_prediction - α7 * score_duplication_risk - α8 * score_constraint_violation_risk - α9 * score_platform_decay_risk ``` ### Definitions ```text score_fragment = intrinsic content strength score_natural_center_match = identity preservation score_platform_fit = structural compatibility with platform-state(t) score_predicted_audience_transition = probability of moving audience to next state score_diversity_contribution = usefulness relative to already-selected units score_economic_yield_prediction = expected follower/lead yield score_duplication_risk = near-duplicate or cannibalized output score_constraint_violation_risk = Natural Center or technical rule risk score_platform_decay_risk = overuse of patterns losing efficacy on current platform ``` --- ## 9. Minimal implementable formulas If you want the shortest path to an actual build, start with this reduced production set. ```python FRAGMENT_SCORE = ( 0.12 * visual_saliency + 0.10 * motion_energy + 0.10 * subject_prominence + 0.08 * audio_energy + 0.12 * hook_density + 0.15 * thematic_alignment + 0.12 * tonal_alignment + 0.08 * narrative_tension + 0.05 * symbolic_marker_density + 0.08 * semantic_novelty ) PLATFORM_SCORE = ( 0.25 * duration_fit + 0.20 * opening_pattern_fit + 0.15 * replayability + 0.15 * subtitle_fit + 0.25 * platform_preference_match ) OUTPUT_SCORE = ( 0.45 * FRAGMENT_SCORE + 0.20 * PLATFORM_SCORE + 0.20 * identity_binding + 0.15 * predicted_transition_score ) ``` Reject any candidate where: ```python tonal_alignment < 0.70 thematic_alignment < 0.65 constraint_violation_risk > 0.20 duplication_similarity > 0.90 ``` --- ## 10. Storage schema for scoring ```sql CREATE TABLE fragment_scores ( fragment_id TEXT, platform TEXT, model_version TEXT, visual_saliency FLOAT, motion_energy FLOAT, subject_prominence FLOAT, audio_energy FLOAT, hook_density FLOAT, thematic_alignment FLOAT, tonal_alignment FLOAT, narrative_tension FLOAT, symbolic_marker_density FLOAT, semantic_novelty FLOAT, platform_fit FLOAT, predicted_transition_score FLOAT, total_score FLOAT, created_at TIMESTAMP ); ``` --- ## 11. Recommended operational order The sources support this build sequence, and it is the correct one. ```text Phase 1: normalization fragment extraction Natural Center derivation rule gating basic scoring Phase 2: platform-fit scoring selection diversity caption generation under NC constraints publishing connectors Phase 3: attribution chain audience-transition modeling experimentation layer adaptive weight updates ``` That order matches the implementation blueprint already present in the corpus. fileciteturn2file1 --- ## Final compressed specification ```text Input: premium multimodal asset corpus Extract: temporal fragments, compositional derivatives, textual hooks, symbolic markers Constrain: Natural Center = thematic_core + aesthetic_signature + tonal_vector + narrative_bias + negative_space + invariants Score: fragment strength identity fit platform fit transition probability economic yield prediction Select: top-N diverse outputs under hard constraints Publish: platform-shaped units Learn: attribute performance back to unit, fragment, asset, and NC version update weights cautiously over time ``` This is the exact algorithmic center of the system you have been sketching. Next, the most productive move is to turn this into a formal `SCORING_SPEC_V1` with typed interfaces, thresholds, and event schemas.

Make sure to include fileciteturn0file14 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. I reviewed the source set. The core document defines the MVP as: one premium asset in, many platform-optimized posts out, then scheduled publishing plus weekly reporting on reach, engagement, and leads. fileciteturn2file0 The later system documents correctly extend that into a research-grade operating system with explicit `assets`, `fragments`, `generated_units`, `publish_events`, and `performance_observations`, and they already sketch the attribution path as `performance → publish_event → unit → fragment → asset`. fileciteturn2file1 The more advanced operator document also makes clear that attribution must not stop at “engagement”; it must connect output to audience movement, economics, and platform state over time. fileciteturn2file2 ## Source-grounded conclusion The mathematically correct attribution model for this system is not single-touch and not purely last-post. It has to be a **hierarchical, time-decayed, multi-touch attribution graph** with five levels: ```text Asset → Fragment → GeneratedUnit → PublishEvent → PerformanceObservation → AudienceStateTransition / EconomicOutcome ``` That is the only structure consistent with the source documents’ architecture, metrics, and stated goal of turning content generation into an economic engine rather than a posting tool. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Formal attribution model Let: ```text a ∈ A # source asset f ∈ F(a) # fragment derived from asset a u ∈ U(f) # generated unit derived from fragment f p ∈ P(u) # publish event for unit u o ∈ O(p) # observation window for publish event p k ∈ K # metric type t # time ``` Define base observed metrics per publish event and time window: ```text y_{p,k,t} ``` Examples: ```text k ∈ {views, watch_time, engagement, followers_gained, leads, revenue} ``` The source material already implies these as the primary and derived metrics: engagement, followers, leads, asset yield, follow conversion, retention ratio, and ROI. fileciteturn2file0turn2file1turn2file2 ## Structural lineage weights Every publish event inherits from an upstream chain. Define deterministic lineage indicators: ```text L_asset(a,p) = 1 if publish event p descends from asset a else 0 L_fragment(f,p) = 1 if p descends from fragment f else 0 L_unit(u,p) = 1 if p is generated from unit u else 0 ``` If a post is composed from multiple fragments or mixed inputs, replace binary lineage with normalized weights: ```text w_{a→p} ≥ 0, Σ_a w_{a→p} = 1 w_{f→p} ≥ 0, Σ_f w_{f→p} = 1 w_{u→p} ≥ 0, Σ_u w_{u→p} = 1 ``` This is necessary because a carousel, remix, or stitched cut can carry more than one source fragment. ## Time-decayed metric accumulation A publish event continues to generate value after publication. Let the contribution of publish event `p` to metric `k` over its full life be: ```text M_{p,k} = ∫_0^T y_{p,k}(τ) · d_k(τ) dτ ``` where `d_k(τ)` is a decay kernel. A practical discrete version: ```text M_{p,k} = Σ_{t=0}^{T} y_{p,k,t} · exp(-λ_k t) ``` where: ```text λ_k > 0 ``` Different metrics should use different decay rates. For example, engagement can decay faster than leads; follower gains may decay slower than likes but faster than attributed revenue. This is aligned with the source demand to model content half-life rather than only first-spike engagement. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Platform-normalized value Because platform conditions differ, raw metrics must be normalized by platform and time regime. Let: ```text z_{p,k} = (M_{p,k} - μ_{platform,format,k,era}) / σ_{platform,format,k,era} ``` Then define a platform-adjusted scalar value for a publish event: ```text V_p = Σ_k α_k · z_{p,k} ``` where `α_k` are strategic weights. Example: ```text α_watch_time > α_likes α_followers_gained > α_comments α_leads > α_followers when commercial objective dominates ``` This is required because the system documents explicitly treat platform state as time-varying and platform-specific. fileciteturn2file2 ## Publish-event attribution to upstream objects Now attribute publish-event value upward. ### Unit attribution ```text Attr(u) = Σ_{p ∈ P(u)} w_{u→p} · V_p ``` ### Fragment attribution ```text Attr(f) = Σ_{p} w_{f→p} · V_p ``` ### Asset attribution ```text Attr(a) = Σ_{p} w_{a→p} · V_p ``` This is the direct generalization of the source’s prototype query: ```sql SELECT asset_id, SUM(engagement) FROM performance_observations JOIN publish_events USING (publish_id) JOIN generated_units USING (unit_id) GROUP BY asset_id; ``` but generalized to weighted, multi-metric, time-decayed, platform-normalized attribution rather than naive summed engagement. fileciteturn2file1 ## Audience-state-transition attribution The more advanced documents redefine the system around audience formation, not just content output. They specify the audience chain: ```text Viewer → Engaged Viewer → Follower → Returning Follower → Converted User → Advocate ``` fileciteturn2file2 So define audience states: ```text S = {0,1,2,3,4,5,6} ``` with transitions: ```text 0→1 unaware→viewer 1→2 viewer→engaged 2→3 engaged→follower 3→4 follower→returning 4→5 returning→converted 5→6 converted→advocate ``` For each publish event `p`, estimate transition counts: ```text T_{p,i→j} ``` Then define transition value weights: ```text β_{i→j} ``` and a state-transition value: ```text Q_p = Σ_{i→j} β_{i→j} · T_{p,i→j} ``` Then total publish-event value becomes: ```text V_p* = V_p + Q_p ``` This lets attribution model not only post performance, but the actual audience movement the sources identify as the system’s core purpose. fileciteturn2file2 ## Economic attribution Let the cost basis for asset `a` be: ```text C_a = asset_production_cost + processing_cost + publishing_cost + overhead_allocated ``` Let the asset-attributed economic returns be: ```text R_a = γ_1 · Attr_followers(a) + γ_2 · Attr_leads(a) + γ_3 · Attr_revenue(a) + γ_4 · Attr_brand_value(a) ``` Then: ```text GrossAssetReturn(a) = R_a NetAssetReturn(a) = R_a - C_a ROI(a) = (R_a - C_a) / C_a AssetYieldMultiplier(a) = R_a / C_a ``` This directly matches the source ambition to move from “beautiful video” to measurable yield, leads, and ROI. fileciteturn2file0turn2file2 ## Causal rather than correlational attribution The source set explicitly says the system must move from correlation to causal inference and treat every post as a micro-experiment. fileciteturn1file0turn2file1 So observational attribution above is necessary but insufficient. Add a causal lift term. For a treatment variant `r` relative to control `c`, define: ```text Lift_{k} = E[Y_k | do(r)] - E[Y_k | do(c)] ``` In randomized deployment: ```text \hat{Lift}_{k} = mean(Y_k | treatment) - mean(Y_k | control) ``` Then fragment-level or unit-level causal value is: ```text CausalAttr(u,k) = n_u · \hat{Lift}_{u,k} ``` where `n_u` is treatment exposure count. For non-randomized deployments, use a doubly robust estimate: ```text DR_k = 1/n Σ_i [ m_1(x_i) - m_0(x_i) + z_i (y_i - m_1(x_i))/e(x_i) - (1-z_i)(y_i - m_0(x_i))/(1-e(x_i)) ] ``` where: ```text z_i = treatment indicator e(x_i) = propensity score m_1, m_0 = outcome models ``` Then use `DR_k` as the causal increment rather than raw observed delta. ## Natural-center constraint in attribution The source set repeatedly insists that every output inherit from a Natural Center and that identity be measurable and enforceable. fileciteturn2file2 So attribution should penalize off-brand wins that damage long-term system coherence. Let: ```text NCScore_p ∈ [0,1] ``` Then define identity-adjusted event value: ```text V_p^NC = V_p* · (1 - ρ + ρ·NCScore_p) ``` where `ρ ∈ [0,1]` controls how strongly identity coherence matters. If `NCScore_p < θ_min`, either reject the unit entirely or discount its attributed value sharply: ```text V_p^NC = 0 if NCScore_p < θ_min_reject ``` This prevents the model from rewarding generic noise that performs temporarily but erodes brand recognizability. ## Full attribution equation The cleanest production equation is: ```text FinalAttr(a) = Σ_p w_{a→p} · [ Σ_k α_k · Norm(M_{p,k}) + Σ_{i→j} β_{i→j} · T_{p,i→j} + CausalLift_p ] · IdentityAdj_p · ReliabilityAdj_p ``` where: ```text Norm(M_{p,k}) = platform/time normalized metric value CausalLift_p = experimental or causal increment IdentityAdj_p = natural-center coherence modifier ReliabilityAdj_p = source reliability modifier for incomplete analytics ``` This is the mathematically correct top-level formula for your system. ## Practical SQL-ready schema logic At minimum, keep these tables: ```sql assets(asset_id, brand_id, cost_basis, created_at) fragments(fragment_id, asset_id, energy_score, semantic_cluster) generated_units(unit_id, fragment_id, platform, unit_type, nc_version) publish_events(publish_id, unit_id, platform, published_at, schedule_policy_id) performance_observations(obs_id, publish_id, observed_at, metric_name, metric_value, reliability) audience_transitions(transition_id, publish_id, from_state, to_state, transition_count) attribution_weights(publish_id, asset_id, fragment_id, unit_id, w_asset, w_fragment, w_unit) experiments(experiment_id, unit_id, control_flag, treatment_flag, hypothesis_id) ``` Then a first non-naive asset attribution view looks like: ```sql WITH metric_rollup AS ( SELECT po.publish_id, SUM( CASE po.metric_name WHEN 'engagement' THEN po.metric_value * 0.20 WHEN 'watch_time' THEN po.metric_value * 0.25 WHEN 'followers_gained' THEN po.metric_value * 0.30 WHEN 'leads' THEN po.metric_value * 0.25 ELSE 0 END * po.reliability ) AS metric_score FROM performance_observations po GROUP BY po.publish_id ), transition_rollup AS ( SELECT at.publish_id, SUM( CASE WHEN at.from_state = 1 AND at.to_state = 2 THEN at.transition_count * 1 WHEN at.from_state = 2 AND at.to_state = 3 THEN at.transition_count * 3 WHEN at.from_state = 3 AND at.to_state = 4 THEN at.transition_count * 4 WHEN at.from_state = 4 AND at.to_state = 5 THEN at.transition_count * 8 WHEN at.from_state = 5 AND at.to_state = 6 THEN at.transition_count * 12 ELSE 0 END ) AS transition_score FROM audience_transitions at GROUP BY at.publish_id ) SELECT aw.asset_id, SUM( aw.w_asset * ( COALESCE(mr.metric_score,0) + COALESCE(tr.transition_score,0) ) ) AS attributed_value FROM attribution_weights aw LEFT JOIN metric_rollup mr ON mr.publish_id = aw.publish_id LEFT JOIN transition_rollup tr ON tr.publish_id = aw.publish_id GROUP BY aw.asset_id; ``` ## Recommended weighting defaults For V1, use explicit priors instead of pretending precision you do not yet have. A reasonable initialization is: ```text α_views = 0.05 α_watch_time = 0.20 α_engagement = 0.15 α_followers_gained = 0.25 α_leads = 0.35 ``` Audience transition weights can start as: ```text β_1→2 = 1 β_2→3 = 3 β_3→4 = 4 β_4→5 = 8 β_5→6 = 12 ``` Then update these from empirical outcome data after enough experiments accumulate. ## What this gives you This model resolves the main deficiencies visible across the source set. It replaces weekly reporting on raw reach and engagement with a lineage-aware economic attribution system. fileciteturn2file0turn2file1 It extends the MVP from “post creation and scheduling” into “measurable audience formation and asset yield.” fileciteturn2file0turn2file2 It makes platform change a variable in the math instead of an external annoyance. fileciteturn2file2 It keeps the system from rewarding generic high-performing but identity-destructive outputs by incorporating Natural Center coherence into attributed value. fileciteturn2file2 ## Bottom line The mathematically correct attribution doctrine for Cronus-Metabolus is: ```text Observed performance → normalized by platform/time → weighted by lineage → expanded by audience-state transitions → corrected by causal lift → discounted by identity drift and data unreliability → rolled up into asset-level economic return ``` That is the model. Next, the logical move is to turn this into a formal `ATTRIBUTION_ENGINE_SPEC` with exact table definitions, metric registry, and estimator choices for V1 versus V2.
