The core product thesis is stable: one premium asset becomes many platform-native outputs with measurable downstream yield. fileciteturn0file15 The operator-grade documents then extend that into a system with Natural Center enforcement, attribution, experimentation, platform intelligence, and event-driven workflows. fileciteturn0file1turn0file0 The correct expansion is therefore: ```text single-brand content engine → multi-tenant SaaS control plane → orchestration-backed execution plane → research / attribution / adaptation plane ``` ## Canonical system identity ```bash $SYSTEM_NAME="cronus-metabolus" $PRODUCT_SURFACE="auto-content-cannibal" $SYSTEM_CLASS="multi-tenant temporal content yield and audience formation SaaS" $ARCHITECTURE_STYLE="control-plane + orchestration-plane + tenant-isolated data-plane" ``` ## What changes when this becomes SaaS The original product is effectively: ```text asset in → fragments → generated posts → scheduling → weekly report ``` fileciteturn0file15 The SaaS form must become: ```text tenant → workspace → brand profiles → assets → orchestration workflows → platform publishing → analytics ingestion → attribution → experiments → reports ``` That implies four new first-class capabilities. First, tenant isolation. Every object in the system must belong to a tenant and optionally to a sub-brand or workspace. Second, orchestration. Every long-running process must run through a durable workflow engine rather than synchronous application logic. The source blueprint already points to Temporal as the correct workflow engine. fileciteturn0file0turn0file1 Third, policy separation. System policy, tenant policy, brand policy, and platform policy must be independently versioned. Fourth, multi-surface control. One control plane for operators and clients; one execution plane for workflows and workers. ## SaaS macro-architecture The clean form is this: ```text [ Web App / API Gateway ] | v [ SaaS Control Plane ] | v [ Orchestration Layer ] | v [ Domain Services ] | v [ Tenant-Isolated Data + Object Storage + Analytics ] ``` ### 1. Control plane This is where humans interact with the system. It should own authentication, billing context, tenant management, workspace configuration, brand setup, approvals, campaign setup, and report access. Minimal applications: ```text operator-console tenant-admin-console reporting-dashboard gateway-api ``` This is a direct expansion of the repo skeleton already implied in the sources. fileciteturn0file13 ### 2. Orchestration layer This is the core addition you asked for. The orchestration layer should not be a thin queue wrapper. It should be the durable stateful execution fabric for all long-lived processes: ```text asset intake workflow media analysis workflow natural center derivation workflow generation workflow approval workflow publishing workflow analytics backfill workflow attribution recompute workflow experiment workflow platform-intelligence refresh workflow ``` The source implementation blueprint already frames the system as event-driven with a workflow engine and an explicit asset pipeline. fileciteturn0file0turn0file1 Recommended identity: ```bash $WORKFLOW_ENGINE="Temporal" $ORCHESTRATION_ROLE="durable execution, retries, compensation, human approval gates, replay" ``` ### 3. Domain services These remain largely the same as in the operator-grade sources, but now they run as tenant-aware services behind orchestration. Canonical services: ```text auth-identity tenant-management billing-metering asset-ingestion media-analysis natural-center generation-orchestrator platform-intelligence scheduling-engine publishing-connectors analytics-ingestion attribution-engine experimentation-engine research-registry notification-service audit-log-service policy-engine ``` The core service map is already source-grounded; tenant-management, billing-metering, notification-service, and audit-log-service are the necessary SaaS completions. fileciteturn0file1turn0file13 ## Multi-tenant domain model The original documents define `Asset`, `Fragment`, `NaturalCenter`, `GeneratedUnit`, `PublishEvent`, `PerformanceObservation`, `Experiment`, and `PlatformProfile`. fileciteturn0file1turn0file14 For SaaS, prepend tenant structure above them: ```text Tenant → Workspace → BrandProfile → NaturalCenter → Asset → Fragment → GeneratedUnit → PublishEvent → PerformanceObservation → Experiment → Report ``` ### Canonical tenancy hierarchy ```yaml Tenant: tenant_id: string name: string plan_id: string status: active|suspended|trial created_at: datetime Workspace: workspace_id: string tenant_id: string name: string region: string created_at: datetime BrandProfile: brand_id: string tenant_id: string workspace_id: string name: string industry: string platform_policy_id: string default_natural_center_id: string TenantUser: user_id: string tenant_id: string workspace_id: string role: owner|admin|editor|analyst|viewer BrandConnection: connection_id: string tenant_id: string brand_id: string platform: youtube|tiktok|linkedin|instagram credential_ref: string status: active|expired|revoked ``` Then all downstream objects must include at minimum: ```text tenant_id workspace_id brand_id ``` This is non-negotiable if the system is to support white-label agencies, enterprise clients, and direct brands simultaneously, which the product thesis explicitly anticipates. fileciteturn0file15 ## Orchestration layer design This layer should model workflows as explicit state machines with tenant-scoped execution context. ### Execution contract Every workflow should carry: ```yaml WorkflowContext: tenant_id: string workspace_id: string brand_id: string actor_id: string correlation_id: string workflow_id: string policy_snapshot_id: string platform_profile_version: string natural_center_version: string ``` ### Core workflow families #### Asset pipeline workflow ```text asset.uploaded → asset.normalized → fragments.extracted → natural_center.loaded_or_derived → candidates.scored → units.generated → approval.requested → publish.plan.created ``` #### Publish workflow ```text publish.plan.created → platform.connector.validated → post.rendered → post.published → publish.confirmed → analytics.poll.scheduled ``` #### Analytics and attribution workflow ```text analytics.poll.started → analytics.raw.ingested → metrics.normalized → attribution.recomputed → audience-transition.updated → report.materialized ``` #### Experiment workflow ```text experiment.created → treatment.assignment.generated → units.variantized → publish.routed → metrics.collected → significance.computed → strategy.updated ``` #### Platform intelligence workflow ```text platform-doc.refresh → schema.diff.detected → capability.delta.detected → platform-profile.revised → tenant-policies.revalidated → alert.emitted ``` These are direct continuations of the workflow and platform-intelligence logic already present in the source set. fileciteturn0file1turn0file0 ## Isolation model A real multi-tenant SaaS needs explicit isolation doctrine. ### Data isolation Recommended default: ```text shared application cluster + shared control-plane services + row-level isolation in operational DB + tenant-scoped object-store prefixes + tenant-scoped warehouse partitions ``` For enterprise plans, add optional logical or physical isolation: ```text dedicated database schema or dedicated deployment cell per tenant ``` ### Storage pattern ```bash $OBJECT_STORE_URI $OBJECT_STORE_BUCKET_RAW $OBJECT_STORE_BUCKET_DERIVED $OBJECT_STORE_BUCKET_REPORTS $TENANT_OBJECT_PREFIX="tenants/$TENANT_ID/" ``` Example pathing: ```text tenants/T_001/brands/B_044/assets/A_938/raw/hero-film.mp4 tenants/T_001/brands/B_044/fragments/F_193/clip_01.mp4 tenants/T_001/brands/B_044/reports/R_2026_03_weekly.pdf ``` ### Security model Every request path must resolve authorization through: ```text user → tenant membership → workspace membership → role → brand permission → action policy ``` And every workflow execution must be auditable. ## Policy architecture The sources already insist on invariant-bearing logic: Natural Center rules, platform assumptions, experimentation rules, and time-bounded platform profiles. fileciteturn0file1turn0file14 In SaaS form, this becomes a layered policy system: ```text system policy → tenant policy → workspace policy → brand policy → campaign policy → platform runtime policy ``` ### Example policy stack ```yaml SystemPolicy: require_natural_center: true max_generated_units_per_asset: 40 TenantPolicy: auto_publish_allowed: false analytics_retention_days: 365 allowed_platforms: [linkedin, instagram] BrandPolicy: tonal_drift_threshold: 0.18 approval_required: true prohibited_formats: [meme] CampaignPolicy: window_days: 30 primary_metric: followers_gained PlatformRuntimePolicy: platform_profile_id: PP_2026_03_25_LINKEDIN confidence_floor: 0.65 ``` This is what keeps multi-tenant flexibility from dissolving system integrity. ## Canonical repository expansion The source repo skeleton is already close. fileciteturn0file13 The SaaS form should become: ```text cronus-metabolus-os/ apps/ gateway-api/ operator-console/ tenant-admin-console/ reporting-dashboard/ public-docs-site/ services/ auth-identity/ tenant-management/ billing-metering/ asset-ingestion/ media-analysis/ natural-center/ generation-orchestrator/ platform-intelligence/ scheduling-engine/ publishing-connectors/ analytics-ingestion/ attribution-engine/ experimentation-engine/ research-registry/ policy-engine/ notification-service/ audit-log-service/ packages/ domain-models/ event-schemas/ workflow-schemas/ metric-definitions/ platform-adapters/ natural-center-sdk/ experiment-sdk/ tenant-sdk/ policy-sdk/ shared-config/ shared-auth/ shared-logging/ infra/ terraform/ kubernetes/ workflows/temporal/ monitoring/ secrets/ data/ db-migrations/ warehouse-models/ schemas/ seeds/ clients/ templates/ tenant-profiles/ brand-profiles/ natural-centers/ campaign-configs/ research/ hypotheses/ experiments/ evidence/ reports/ platform-change-log/ contracts/ api/ events/ database/ metrics/ workflows/ policies/ ``` ## Database completion for SaaS You already have the operator-grade tables. fileciteturn0file0turn0file14 For SaaS, add tenancy and orchestration metadata. ```sql CREATE TABLE tenants ( tenant_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, plan_id TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE workspaces ( workspace_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(tenant_id), name TEXT NOT NULL, region TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE brand_profiles ( brand_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(tenant_id), workspace_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workspaces(workspace_id), name TEXT NOT NULL, industry TEXT, default_natural_center_id TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE tenant_users ( user_id TEXT NOT NULL, tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(tenant_id), workspace_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workspaces(workspace_id), role TEXT NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY (user_id, tenant_id, workspace_id) ); ALTER TABLE assets ADD COLUMN tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE assets ADD COLUMN workspace_id TEXT NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE assets ADD COLUMN brand_id TEXT NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE fragments ADD COLUMN tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE generated_units ADD COLUMN tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE publish_events ADD COLUMN tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE performance_observations ADD COLUMN tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL; ALTER TABLE experiments ADD COLUMN tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL; CREATE TABLE workflow_runs ( workflow_run_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL, workspace_id TEXT NOT NULL, brand_id TEXT NOT NULL, workflow_type TEXT NOT NULL, workflow_state TEXT NOT NULL, correlation_id TEXT NOT NULL, started_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, completed_at TIMESTAMP ); CREATE TABLE policy_snapshots ( policy_snapshot_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL, workspace_id TEXT, brand_id TEXT, policy_type TEXT NOT NULL, policy_json JSONB NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE audit_log ( audit_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL, actor_id TEXT NOT NULL, entity_type TEXT NOT NULL, entity_id TEXT NOT NULL, action TEXT NOT NULL, payload JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL ); ``` ## Environment-variable contract Per your standing preference, the system should be parameterized from the start. ```bash $APP_ENV= $SYSTEM_ID=cronus-metabolus $SYSTEM_CLASS=multi-tenant-temporal-content-yield-saas $PUBLIC_APP_URI= $GATEWAY_PORT=3000 $OPERATOR_CONSOLE_PORT=3001 $TENANT_ADMIN_PORT=3002 $REPORTING_DASHBOARD_PORT=3003 $POSTGRES_URI= $REDIS_URI= $EVENT_BUS_URI= $WORKFLOW_ENGINE_URI= $WORKFLOW_NAMESPACE=cronus-metabolus $OBJECT_STORE_URI= $DATA_WAREHOUSE_URI= $MODEL_REGISTRY_URI= $EVIDENCE_STORE_URI= $MULTI_TENANT_MODE=true $DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1 $TENANT_ISOLATION_MODE=row_level $ENTERPRISE_DEDICATED_CELL_ENABLED=true $JWT_ISSUER= $JWT_AUDIENCE= $JWT_PRIVATE_KEY= $JWT_PUBLIC_KEY= $SESSION_SECRET= $ENCRYPTION_KEY= $OPENAI_API_KEY= $EMBEDDING_MODEL_ID= $GENERATION_MODEL_ID= $VISION_MODEL_ID= $TRANSCRIPTION_MODEL_ID= $YOUTUBE_CONNECTOR_ENABLED=false $TIKTOK_CONNECTOR_ENABLED=false $LINKEDIN_CONNECTOR_ENABLED=false $INSTAGRAM_CONNECTOR_ENABLED=false $YOUTUBE_CLIENT_ID= $YOUTUBE_CLIENT_SECRET= $TIKTOK_CLIENT_ID= $TIKTOK_CLIENT_SECRET= $LINKEDIN_CLIENT_ID= $LINKEDIN_CLIENT_SECRET= $INSTAGRAM_CLIENT_ID= $INSTAGRAM_CLIENT_SECRET= $NATURAL_CENTER_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD=0.82 $TONAL_DRIFT_THRESHOLD=0.18 $EXPERIMENT_MIN_SAMPLE_SIZE=1000 $SIGNIFICANCE_ALPHA=0.05 $PLATFORM_SHIFT_ALERT_THRESHOLD=0.20 $ASSET_RETIREMENT_HALF_LIFE_DAYS=21 $BILLING_PROVIDER=stripe $TRIAL_DAYS=14 $USAGE_METERING_ENABLED=true $MAX_BRANDS_PER_TENANT=10 $MAX_USERS_PER_WORKSPACE=25 $MAX_ASSETS_PER_MONTH=100 $MAX_GENERATED_UNITS_PER_ASSET=40 ``` ## SaaS packaging model The source product thesis already suggests three buyer classes: direct brands, agencies, and white-label buyers. fileciteturn0file15 That naturally becomes three tenancy modes. ```text Mode A: Direct brand tenant Mode B: Agency tenant with many client workspaces Mode C: White-label tenant with custom domain / theming / hidden operator brand ``` ### Commercial packaging ```text Starter: 1 tenant 1 workspace 1 brand fixed asset cap report access manual approvals Growth: multi-brand more connectors experiments attribution API access Agency: many workspaces white-label reporting delegated access approval chains Enterprise: dedicated cell advanced audit SSO custom retention policy dedicated platform-policy tuning ``` ## What the orchestration layer specifically solves Without orchestration, the system becomes brittle in exactly the places the source documents care about most: long-running media jobs, retries, publish recovery, analytics backfills, experiment assignment, and platform-state adaptation. fileciteturn0file0turn0file1 With orchestration, you get: ```text durable asset pipelines retry-safe connector execution human-in-the-loop approval steps workflow replay tenant-aware auditability scheduled backfills policy snapshotting cross-service consistency ``` That is why the orchestration layer is not optional middleware. It is the execution spine. ## Final synthesis The correct SaaS evolution is: ```text auto-content-cannibal = market-facing product surface cronus-metabolus = multi-tenant orchestration-backed operating system underneath ``` And the correct system law set remains: ```text Every output inherits from a Natural Center. Every workflow executes under tenant and policy context. Every campaign is attributable to source assets. Every optimization is experimentally legible. Every platform assumption is time-bounded and revisable. ``` Those laws are already present in the source logic; the SaaS expansion simply makes them deployable at scale. fileciteturn0file1turn0file0turn0file13 The next logical artifact is a full `MULTI_TENANT_SAAS_SPEC_V1` with service contracts, workflow definitions, table schemas, and tenancy policies.

Make sure to include fileciteturn0file16 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. Reviewed. The source set already fixes the role of Natural Center as a **computable identity constraint system** and places it between extraction and generation, not as an afterthought. It is the object that makes identity measurable, enforceable, and optimizable, and every downstream output is supposed to inherit from it. fileciteturn1file9 fileciteturn1file15 ## Formal definition Let the Natural Center for brand or corpus \(B\) be: \[ NC(B)=\langle T,A,\Tau,N,S,\mathcal{I},\mathcal{M},E\rangle \] where: \[ T = \text{thematic core} \] \[ A = \text{aesthetic signature} \] \[ \Tau = \text{tonal vector} \] \[ N = \text{narrative bias} \] \[ S = \text{symbolic markers} \] \[ \mathcal{I} = \text{invariants} \] \[ \mathcal{M} = \text{mutation bounds} \] \[ E = \text{embedding} \] This structure is already aligned with the source object definition: thematic core, aesthetic signature, tonal vector, narrative bias, symbolic markers, negative space, invariants, mutation bounds, and an embedding representation. fileciteturn2file0 I would tighten it by making **negative space** first-class and explicit: \[ NC(B)=\langle T,A,\Tau,N,S,\Omega,\mathcal{I},\mathcal{M},E\rangle \] where \(\Omega\) is the set of prohibited features, tones, structures, and motifs. ## System position The source logic fixes the global relation as: \[ content = f(asset,\ natural\_center,\ platform\_profile) \] and therefore Natural Center extraction is not a decorative metadata task; it is a constraint-derivation task that conditions generation and scoring. fileciteturn1file16 So the correct pipeline is: ```text asset normalization → multimodal fragment extraction → Natural Center derivation → constraint gating → platform-aware generation → audience-transition scoring → attribution + learning ``` That sequencing is already explicit in the source build order. fileciteturn2file0 ## Exact extraction algorithm I would formalize the Natural Center subsystem as `ALG_NC_01`. ### Stage 0: corpus assembly Input corpus \(C\) should be tenant- and brand-scoped: \[ C = C_v \cup C_i \cup C_a \cup C_t \cup C_h \] where: \[ C_v = \text{video assets} \] \[ C_i = \text{image / render assets} \] \[ C_a = \text{audio assets} \] \[ C_t = \text{text / voice corpus} \] \[ C_h = \text{historical high-performing outputs, if available} \] If only one premium asset exists, the sources still allow `NATURAL_CENTER_MIN_ASSETS=1`, so bootstrap from a single asset is valid, but confidence must be lower and human review should remain enabled. fileciteturn1file2 ### Stage 1: multimodal feature extraction For each asset \(x \in C\), compute feature families. For visual inputs: \[ \phi_v(x)=\langle color,\ composition,\ framing,\ motion,\ texture,\ saliency,\ object\_presence\rangle \] For audio inputs: \[ \phi_a(x)=\langle rms,\ spectral\_flux,\ tempo,\ silence,\ emphasis,\ prosody\rangle \] For text and speech: \[ \phi_t(x)=\langle lexical\_density,\ sentence\_length,\ rhetoric,\ polarity,\ modality,\ authority,\ abstraction\rangle \] This matches the source extraction logic: color histograms, motion stats, composition maps, lexical density, sentence-length distribution, rhetoric markers, semantic embeddings, and symbolic recurrence. fileciteturn2file0 ### Stage 2: semantic embedding and clustering Embed all semantic-bearing units: \[ e_i = \text{Embed}(u_i) \] where \(u_i\) may be frame captions, transcript spans, textual paragraphs, OCR snippets, or historical post bodies. Cluster embeddings: \[ \mathcal{K} = \text{Cluster}(\{e_i\}) \] For each cluster \(k\), compute: \[ mass(k)=|k| \] \[ cohesion(k)=\frac{1}{|k|}\sum_{e_j\in k}\cos(e_j,\mu_k) \] \[ stability(k)=presence(k)\cdot cohesion(k) \] Select top thematic clusters: \[ T = TopK(\mathcal{K},\ score = stability,\ k=3..5) \] This is directly consistent with the source rule that candidate themes are clustered first and then only stable top-\(k\) themes are retained. fileciteturn2file0 ### Stage 3: aesthetic centroid From visual features across corpus, compute a weighted centroid: \[ A = \frac{\sum_i w_i \phi_v(x_i)}{\sum_i w_i} \] Weights should privilege canonical assets over derivatives: \[ w_i = \begin{cases} 1.0 & \text{hero asset} \\ 0.6 & \text{supporting asset} \\ 0.4 & \text{historical output} \end{cases} \] Decompose \(A\) into subcomponents: \[ A=\langle palette,\ framing,\ motion,\ texture,\ contrast,\ density\rangle \] This stabilizes the visual attractor rather than overfitting to any single frame. ### Stage 4: tonal centroid From linguistic and speech features, compute tonal axes: \[ \Tau=\langle formal,\ expressive,\ emotional,\ authoritative,\ playful\rangle \] Operationally: \[ \Tau = \frac{\sum_i w_i \phi_t(x_i)}{\sum_i w_i} \] Then normalize each axis to \([0,1]\). This matches the source notion of a tonal vector and the later use of tonal alignment as a constraint variable in fragment and output scoring. fileciteturn2file0turn1file13 ### Stage 5: narrative bias extraction Define a narrative pattern function over semantic units: \[ n(u)=\langle transformation,\ tension,\ reveal,\ aspiration,\ proof,\ authority\rangle \] Aggregate across corpus: \[ N = \frac{\sum_i w_i n(u_i)}{\sum_i w_i} \] This gives you a persistent bias profile. For example, some brands resolve toward proof and authority; others toward transformation and aspiration. ### Stage 6: symbolic marker discovery Let \(m_j\) be recurring symbolic candidates, visual or textual. For each candidate compute: \[ symbolic\_score(m_j)=frequency(m_j)\cdot distinctiveness(m_j)\cdot consistency(m_j) \] Accept into symbolic marker set \(S\) if: \[ symbolic\_score(m_j)\ge \tau_{symbol} \] The source env contract already suggests a production threshold, `SYMBOLIC_MARKER_MIN_SCORE=0.70`. fileciteturn1file2 ### Stage 7: negative-space derivation This is critical. The source set correctly frames negative space as “what the system must never produce.” fileciteturn0file32 Formally, define the universal feature pool \(U\) for your generation domain. Then: \[ \Omega = U \setminus H \] where \(H\) is the set of consistently present, accepted, or high-confidence brand-aligned features. In practice, compute a prohibited-feature score: \[ forbid(z)=absence(z)\cdot incompatibility(z)\cdot drift\_risk(z) \] and include \(z\) in negative space if: \[ forbid(z)\ge \tau_{\Omega} \] Examples include meme tonality, bright-saturated palette, slang, jump-cut overload, stock-motivation rhetoric, or comedic framing. ### Stage 8: invariant induction Convert stable feature patterns into enforceable rules. For each candidate feature \(q\), compute: \[ stability(q)=presence\_across\_assets(q)\cdot consistency\_across\_assets(q) \] This stability rule is already specified in the source material. fileciteturn2file0 Accept as invariant if: \[ stability(q)\ge \tau_{inv} \] Then emit rule forms such as: ```text all outputs must express at least one thematic_core element all captions must remain within tonal distance threshold all visual outputs must remain inside palette bounds no output may violate negative_space ``` ### Stage 9: mutation-bound estimation Mutation bounds determine how far outputs may vary while remaining “of the same identity.” For each feature family \(d\), estimate historical variance: \[ \sigma_d^2 = Var_d(C) \] Set allowed mutation bound: \[ \mathcal{M}_d = [\mu_d - \lambda\sigma_d,\ \mu_d + \lambda\sigma_d] \] with lower \(\lambda\) for identity-critical dimensions and higher \(\lambda\) for expressive dimensions. A reasonable first partition is: ```text palette / tone / symbolic recurrence: low mutation hook structure / pacing / CTA framing: medium mutation platform packaging details: high mutation ``` ### Stage 10: embedding synthesis Finally, build a center embedding: \[ E = \text{Fuse}(Embed(T), Embed(A), Embed(\Tau), Embed(N), Embed(S)) \] where `Fuse` may be concatenation followed by learned projection, or weighted averaging into a shared latent space. This produces a single vector against which downstream items can be scored for Natural Center similarity. ## Confidence model The missing precision in the earlier drafts is confidence accounting. You need it. Define: \[ conf(NC)=g(n_{assets}, modality\_coverage, feature\_stability, review\_status) \] A simple V1 form: \[ conf(NC)=0.35\cdot assets\_score + 0.25\cdot modality\_score + 0.25\cdot stability\_score + 0.15\cdot review\_score \] This matters because the sources allow single-asset bootstrap, but single-asset NC should not claim the same certainty as a mature multi-asset corpus. fileciteturn1file2 ## Downstream enforcement Once extracted, the Natural Center must act as a gate. For any fragment or generated unit \(y\), compute: \[ NC\_match(y)=\alpha_1\cdot thematic\_alignment +\alpha_2\cdot tonal\_alignment +\alpha_3\cdot aesthetic\_match +\alpha_4\cdot symbolic\_marker\_density \] Those component scores are already defined in the source scoring layer. fileciteturn1file13turn2file0 Then apply hard gating: ```text if violates_negative_space(y, NC): reject if tonal_alignment(y) < τ_tone: reject if NC_match(y) < τ_nc: reject ``` This is exactly how the source system already treats negative space and tonal alignment in fragment gating. fileciteturn1file13 ## Recommended production thresholds Use the existing source defaults as the V1 baseline: ```bash $NATURAL_CENTER_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD=0.82 $TONAL_DRIFT_THRESHOLD=0.18 $AESTHETIC_DRIFT_THRESHOLD=0.20 $SYMBOLIC_MARKER_MIN_SCORE=0.70 $NEGATIVE_SPACE_ENFORCEMENT=true $IDENTITY_BLOCK_ON_DRIFT=true ``` These are already present in the repository architecture spec and should remain the policy defaults until empirical tuning improves them. fileciteturn1file2 ## Versioned object schema I would store the Natural Center as: ```json { "nc_id": "NC_<hash>", "tenant_id": "T_*", "workspace_id": "W_*", "brand_id": "B_*", "version": 1, "confidence_score": 0.74, "thematic_core": ["t1", "t2", "t3", "t4"], "aesthetic_signature": { "palette": ["..."], "composition": ["..."], "motion": ["..."], "texture": ["..."] }, "tonal_vector": { "formal": 0.81, "expressive": 0.34, "emotional": 0.42, "authoritative": 0.77, "playful": 0.11 }, "narrative_bias": { "transformation": 0.69, "tension": 0.48, "reveal": 0.56, "aspiration": 0.73, "proof": 0.61 }, "symbolic_markers": ["..."], "negative_space": ["..."], "invariants": ["..."], "mutation_bounds": { "tone": 0.18, "aesthetic": 0.20 }, "embedding": ["..."], "created_at": "..." } ``` ## Final compact specification The formal extraction contract is: \[ NC = ALG\_NC\_01(C) \] with \[ ALG\_NC\_01 = derive\_themes \circ derive\_aesthetics \circ derive\_tone \circ derive\_narrative \circ derive\_symbols \circ derive\_negative\_space \circ induce\_invariants \circ estimate\_mutation\_bounds \circ encode \] and acceptance criterion: \[ NC\ \text{is valid iff}\ conf(NC)\ge \tau_{conf} \] while any downstream output \(y\) is valid iff: \[ NC\_match(y)\ge \tau_{nc} \quad\land\quad drift(y)\le \tau_{drift} \quad\land\quad \neg violates\_negative\_space(y) \] That is the correct formalization. The next artifact should be a strict `NATURAL_CENTER_SPEC_V1` with typed interfaces, table schema, and validator functions.

## Assistant (artifact)

Make sure to include fileciteturn0file17 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. The sources converge on a consistent requirement: > **“Natural Center” already exists as a constraint object. What is missing is its projection into a *computable embedding space* that can be used uniformly across extraction, generation, scoring, attribution, and evolution.** Below is the **brand embedding structure**—formalized to integrate directly into your existing pipeline (Natural Center, fragment scoring, platform adaptation, attribution). --- # I. POSITION IN SYSTEM From your sources: ```text content = f(asset, natural_center, platform_profile) ``` Extend this to: ```text content = f(asset, brand_embedding, platform_profile) ``` Where: ```text brand_embedding = encode(NaturalCenter) ``` This becomes the **single vectorized control surface** for: - generation constraints - similarity validation - scoring alignment - attribution weighting - drift detection The Natural Center is the **symbolic object**. The brand embedding is the **computational interface**. --- # II. FORMAL OBJECT Let: \[ BE(B) \in \mathbb{R}^d \] Where: ```text BE = Brand Embedding d = embedding dimensionality (e.g. 768, 1024, 1536) ``` Constructed as: \[ BE = \Phi(T, A, \Tau, N, S, \Omega) \] From sources: - \(T\): thematic_core - \(A\): aesthetic_signature - \(\Tau\): tonal_vector - \(N\): narrative_bias - \(S\): symbolic_markers - \(\Omega\): negative_space fileciteturn0file16 --- # III. MULTI-VECTOR DECOMPOSITION (CRITICAL) Do **not** use a single monolithic embedding. Use a structured embedding: ```yaml $BRAND_EMBEDDING: semantic_vector: ℝ^d_s visual_vector: ℝ^d_v tonal_vector: ℝ^d_t narrative_vector: ℝ^d_n symbolic_vector: ℝ^d_sym negative_space_vector: ℝ^d_neg fused_vector: ℝ^d_f ``` --- ## 1. Semantic Vector ```text semantic_vector = mean(Embed(text_units)) ``` Source: - transcript - captions - brand copy - semantic clusters Represents: ```text WHAT the brand is about ``` --- ## 2. Visual Vector ```text visual_vector = mean(Embed(frames / crops)) ``` Source: - keyframes - crops - render sets Represents: ```text HOW the brand looks ``` --- ## 3. Tonal Vector ```text tonal_vector = projection(linguistic_features) ``` Axes: ```text formal ↔ casual minimal ↔ expressive cold ↔ emotional authoritative ↔ conversational ``` Represents: ```text HOW the brand speaks ``` --- ## 4. Narrative Vector ```text narrative_vector = distribution over: transformation tension reveal aspiration proof ``` Represents: ```text HOW the brand structures meaning ``` --- ## 5. Symbolic Vector ```text symbolic_vector = embedding(symbolic_markers) ``` Includes: - motifs - recurring visual patterns - repeated phrases Represents: ```text RECOGNITION layer ``` --- ## 6. Negative Space Vector This is the **non-obvious but critical component**. ```text negative_space_vector = embedding(prohibited_features) ``` Examples: - no humor - no meme structure - no bright palette - no slang Represents: ```text BOUNDARY of identity ``` --- ## 7. Fused Vector Final embedding: ```text fused_vector = W_s * semantic_vector + W_v * visual_vector + W_t * tonal_vector + W_n * narrative_vector + W_sym * symbolic_vector - W_neg * negative_space_vector ``` Where: ```text Σ weights = 1 ``` This subtraction is important: ```text negative space actively repels invalid outputs ``` --- # IV. STORAGE SCHEMA ```json { "brand_id": "B_*", "nc_id": "NC_*", "version": 1, "embedding": { "semantic": [...], "visual": [...], "tonal": [...], "narrative": [...], "symbolic": [...], "negative": [...], "fused": [...] }, "weights": { "semantic": 0.20, "visual": 0.20, "tonal": 0.15, "narrative": 0.15, "symbolic": 0.15, "negative": 0.15 }, "confidence_score": 0.74, "created_at": "..." } ``` --- # V. CORE OPERATIONS ## 1. Similarity ```text similarity(x) = cosine(Embed(x), fused_vector) ``` Used for: - validation - scoring - filtering --- ## 2. Multi-Dimensional Alignment Instead of single score: ```text alignment(x) = { semantic: cosine(x_s, semantic_vector), visual: cosine(x_v, visual_vector), tonal: cosine(x_t, tonal_vector), narrative: cosine(x_n, narrative_vector), symbolic: cosine(x_sym, symbolic_vector) } ``` --- ## 3. Negative Constraint Check ```text neg_score(x) = cosine(x, negative_space_vector) if neg_score > τ_neg: reject ``` --- ## 4. Final Brand Score ```text brand_score(x) = Σ_i w_i * alignment_i - w_neg * neg_score ``` --- # VI. INTEGRATION POINTS ## 1. Fragment Scoring (existing system) Extend: ```text fragment_score = intrinsic_score + brand_score(fragment) ``` --- ## 2. Generation Constraints ```text LLM(prompt + embedding conditioning) ``` or: ```text generate until: brand_score(output) ≥ τ ``` --- ## 3. Attribution Weighting From your attribution model: ```text V_p^NC = V_p * identity_factor ``` Now define: ```text identity_factor = brand_score(output) ``` --- ## 4. Drift Detection ```text drift = 1 - cosine(output_embedding, fused_vector) if drift > threshold: trigger identity_drift_event ``` --- ## 5. Evolution Loop ```text BE(t+1) = BE(t) + α * mean(high_performers) - β * mean(low_performers) ``` Constraint: ```text ||BE(t+1) - BE(t)|| < ε ``` Prevents identity collapse. --- # VII. FAILURE MODES (FROM SOURCES, NOW FORMALIZED) ### 1. Over-generalization ```text variance(BE) too high → generic outputs ``` ### 2. Over-constraining ```text variance(BE) too low → no creative space ``` ### 3. Drift ```text feedback updates overpower base embedding ``` ### 4. PHASE 2 — ATTRIBUTION + EXPERIMENTATION (Week 7–10) ### Objective Make system scientifically valid. --- ## 1. $SERVICE_ATTRIBUTION_ENGINE ### Function ```bash id="e3nfbx" $FUNC = map( performance → publish_event → unit → fragment → asset ) ``` --- ### Core Query ```sql id="zfakp7" SELECT asset_id, SUM(engagement) FROM performance_observations JOIN publish_events USING (publish_id) JOIN generated_units USING (unit_id) GROUP BY asset_id; ``` --- ## 2. $SERVICE_EXPERIMENTATION_ENGINE ### Experiment Schema ```yaml id="m6qzlz" experiment: id: EXP001 type: hook_test control: variant_A treatment: variant_B metric: retention_3s sample_size: 1000 ``` --- ### Assignment Logic ```bash id="pu2zm1" assign viewers randomly → control/treatment ``` --- ### Significance ```bash id="4449zy" if p_value < $SIGNIFICANCE_ALPHA: accept_result ``` --- # IV. Make sure to include fileciteturn1file1 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. Yes. The operator layer should now be built as a **production research-and-deployment system**, not a mere posting tool. And your platform-evolution requirement is correct: the system must treat platform conditions as a **time-varying environment**, because the success function on YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, and LinkedIn is not stable across eras. Official sources already expose some of the observable surfaces you need to model: YouTube provides channel- and video-level analytics such as audience retention, impressions CTR, watch time, and new/casual/regular viewer segmentation; TikTok exposes posting workflows and developer constraints through its Content Posting APIs and guidelines; LinkedIn exposes organic posting primitives and some company-level engagement data via its marketing APIs. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) # $OPERATOR_GRADE_SYSTEM = "CRONUS_METABOLUS_OS" ```bash $MODE = "full-stack system (multi-service, production-grade)" $SYSTEM_NAME = "Cronus-Metabolus" $SYSTEM_CLASS = "Temporal Content Yield + Audience Formation Operating System" $PRIMARY_GOAL = "Convert premium assets into compounding audience, attribution, and platform-adaptive growth" $SECONDARY_GOAL = "Continuously research, detect, and adapt to platform-state changes over time" ``` # I. What this operator layer actually is This layer is the executable counterpart to the dissertation layer. The dissertation layer defines the invariants, causal claims, theoretical mappings, and research questions. The operator layer turns those into a running system that does six things at once: ingest assets, generate outputs, distribute them, measure results, run experiments, and update its understanding of both audience behavior and platform change. Formally: ```text Asset → Natural Center Extraction → Platform-Aware Generation → Scheduling + Distribution → Analytics + Attribution → Experimentation + Causal Analysis → Platform Change Detection → Model / Rule Updates → Repeat ``` # II. Production-grade service map The correct architecture is multi-service. Monolith first would be faster, but it will fail the research requirement, because research observability, platform adapters, experimentation, and identity enforcement need separate contracts. ```bash $SERVICES = { gateway_api, auth_identity, asset_ingestion, media_analysis, natural_center, generation_orchestrator, platform_intelligence, scheduling_engine, publishing_connectors, analytics_ingestion, attribution_engine, experimentation_engine, research_registry, reporting_dashboard, model_registry, event_bus, data_warehouse, object_storage, workflow_orchestrator, alerting_observability } ``` ## 1. `$SERVICE_GATEWAY_API` This is the system edge. It accepts user, client, asset, campaign, and experiment requests. It should expose stable contracts to UI, internal operators, and later partner integrations. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Entry point for all control-plane requests" $OUTPUTS = "campaign creation, asset registration, experiment submission, report fetch" ``` ## 2. `$SERVICE_ASSET_INGESTION` This service normalizes source material. It accepts hero films, render sets, stills, transcripts, briefing docs, and brand corpus. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "File intake, metadata extraction, transcode initiation, checksum, lineage" ``` ## 3. `$SERVICE_MEDIA_ANALYSIS` This is the decomposition engine in production form. It performs scene detection, keyframe extraction, transcript alignment, pacing analysis, energy-point detection, and content segmentation. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Turn one dense asset into analyzable fragments" ``` ## 4. `$SERVICE_NATURAL_CENTER` This service computes and stores the identity core. It should enforce symbolic, tonal, aesthetic, and thematic invariants across all outputs. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Compute, version, validate, and evolve the Natural Center object" ``` ## 5. `$SERVICE_GENERATION_ORCHESTRATOR` This service creates the outputs. It composes platform-specific assets, caption variants, CTA variants, sequencing plans, and campaign bundles. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Generate platform-native outputs constrained by identity and platform profile" ``` ## 6. `$SERVICE_PLATFORM_INTELLIGENCE` This is now mandatory. It models each platform as a time-dependent system. It should ingest official platform documentation changes, API changes, available analytics fields, posting constraints, and internal performance shifts. YouTube’s analytics surfaces already include audience retention, traffic and audience segments, while TikTok and LinkedIn expose developer posting capabilities and versioning or access constraints through official docs; those should be treated as structured inputs to this service. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Track platform state, detect change, update generation and scheduling policy" ``` ## 7. `$SERVICE_SCHEDULING_ENGINE` This service determines when each output should be published, republished, remixed, or retired. It consumes audience timing, asset decay models, and platform profile data. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Temporal orchestration" ``` ## 8. `$SERVICE_PUBLISHING_CONNECTORS` This is the execution layer. It handles the actual post creation and retrieval integrations. TikTok’s Content Posting APIs support direct posting or draft upload flows; LinkedIn’s Posts API supports creation and retrieval of organic and sponsored posts, with content-type constraints and versioned API requirements. ([developers.tiktok.com](https://developers.tiktok.com/products/content-posting-api?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Platform-specific publish / fetch adapters" ``` ## 9. `$SERVICE_ANALYTICS_INGESTION` This service pulls analytics and normalizes them into a common schema. YouTube official analytics surfaces support view, engagement, retention, CTR, and audience segmentation at varying levels, though some data may be limited depending on thresholds and filters. That limitation itself must be modeled as a reliability constraint. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Collect raw performance telemetry and normalize it" ``` ## 10. `$SERVICE_ATTRIBUTION_ENGINE` This is where the product stops being “content software” and becomes an economic system. It traces every post, fragment, caption, CTA, campaign, and asset back to origin. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Asset-level and fragment-level yield accounting" ``` ## 11. `$SERVICE_EXPERIMENTATION_ENGINE` This service owns A/B tests, bandits, holdouts, control/treatment assignment, confidence intervals, and causal inference workflow. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Statistically disciplined optimization" ``` ## 12. `$SERVICE_RESEARCH_REGISTRY` This is the bridge between dissertation-grade and operator-grade. Every hypothesis, paper mapping, experiment, metric definition, and platform-state memo should live here. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Research memory and epistemic governance" ``` ## 13. `$SERVICE_REPORTING_DASHBOARD` This serves operators, clients, and future investors. It must expose asset yield, audience movement, platform differences, and trend drift. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Human-readable operational truth" ``` # III. Canonical data domains You need four data planes, not one. ```bash $DATA_PLANES = { source_plane, operational_plane, experimental_plane, evolutionary_plane } ``` ## `$SOURCE_PLANE` This contains hero assets, renders, transcripts, briefs, brand corpus, prior posts, style references. ## `$OPERATIONAL_PLANE` This contains generated posts, publish events, platform IDs, schedules, revisions, connector logs. ## `$EXPERIMENTAL_PLANE` This contains hypotheses, treatments, controls, randomization groups, test windows, significance results. ## `$EVOLUTIONARY_PLANE` This contains platform snapshots, platform doc changes, field additions/removals, policy updates, performance regime shifts, and internal adaptation rules. # IV. Core database objects The system should be object-oriented at the ontology level and event-driven at the execution level. ```yaml Asset: asset_id: string source_uri: string asset_type: video|image_set|render_set|document brand_id: string checksum: string created_at: datetime Fragment: fragment_id: string asset_id: string fragment_type: clip|frame|crop|layout start_ms: int end_ms: int energy_score: float semantic_cluster: string NaturalCenter: nc_id: string brand_id: string thematic_core: json tonal_vector: json aesthetic_signature: json symbolic_markers: json invariants: json mutation_bounds: json version: int PlatformProfile: profile_id: string platform: youtube|instagram|tiktok|linkedin effective_from: date effective_to: date|null format_weights: json known_constraints: json ranking_assumptions: json confidence_score: float GeneratedUnit: unit_id: string fragment_id: string platform: string unit_type: short_video|carousel|image_post|text_post caption_variant_id: string cta_variant_id: string nc_version: int platform_profile_id: string PublishEvent: publish_id: string unit_id: string platform: string external_post_id: string published_at: datetime schedule_policy_id: string PerformanceObservation: obs_id: string publish_id: string observed_at: datetime metrics: json source_reliability: float Experiment: experiment_id: string hypothesis_id: string treatment_policy: json control_policy: json start_at: datetime end_at: datetime status: draft|running|complete PlatformChangeEvent: change_id: string platform: string detected_at: datetime change_type: api|analytics_field|format|policy|behavioral_shift evidence_uri: string effect_hypothesis: string severity: low|medium|high ``` # V. Platform evolution as a first-class subsystem This is the part most teams omit. It should not be “notes in a document.” It should be a live intelligence loop. ```bash $SUBSYSTEM_PLATFORM_EVOLUTION = { documentation_monitor, analytics_schema_monitor, posting_capability_monitor, performance_regime_monitor, historical_phase_model } ``` ## `$DOCUMENTATION_MONITOR` This tracks official docs, release notes, and API version changes. LinkedIn’s official docs already show versioned API behavior and deprecation notices, which is exactly the type of change this monitor must capture. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/linkedin/marketing/community-management/shares/posts-api?view=li-lms-2026-01&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$ANALYTICS_SCHEMA_MONITOR` This tracks whether a platform adds, removes, or changes analytics surfaces. On YouTube, limited-data conditions must be tracked because they alter what inference is valid. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$POSTING_CAPABILITY_MONITOR` This tracks what formats and workflows are currently supported. TikTok’s direct-post versus draft-upload modes and LinkedIn’s content-type support are examples. ([developers.tiktok.com](https://developers.tiktok.com/products/content-posting-api?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$PERFORMANCE_REGIME_MONITOR` This is internal. It detects when yesterday’s winning structures stop winning. Example: same asset class, same audience band, same cadence, but retention or follow-conversion drops across a time window. ## `$HISTORICAL_PHASE_MODEL` This stores platform eras. That lets you encode your point directly: ```yaml PlatformEra: platform: youtube era_id: YT_2010_CREATOR_GRAPH start_date: 2008-01-01 end_date: 2012-12-31 dominant_mechanism: "subscriber/personality pull" success_features: ["personality continuity", "episode loyalty", "creator identity"] ``` Then later: ```yaml PlatformEra: platform: youtube era_id: YT_2020_RETENTION_RECOMMENDER start_date: 2019-01-01 end_date: null dominant_mechanism: "recommendation + retention + multi-format coexistence" success_features: ["hook quality", "retention curve", "packaging", "viewer segmentation"] ``` The exact dates should remain provisional research objects unless directly established by your source methodology. The point is structural: **platform-state(t)** must exist as a model variable. # VI. The research operating system inside production The system must preserve dissertation-grade rigor while running. ```bash $RESEARCH_OS = { hypothesis_registry, source_registry, experiment_registry, evidence_store, conclusion_registry } ``` ## `$HYPOTHESIS_REGISTRY` Every important claim gets an ID. ```yaml Hypothesis: hypothesis_id: HYP_000127 title: "Open-loop short-form sequencing increases return-viewer ratio on platform X" pillar: behavioral_response platform_scope: ["youtube","tiktok"] temporal_scope: "current" status: proposed|running|supported|rejected ``` ## `$SOURCE_REGISTRY` This stores both secondary and primary sources. Secondary sources support theory. Primary sources support market and platform standing. For platform standing, official platform docs and analytics documentation qualify as primary sources for platform capabilities and measurement surfaces. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$EXPERIMENT_REGISTRY` This records every test and its outcome. ## `$EVIDENCE_STORE` This stores screenshots, exports, API payloads, retention curves, docs snapshots, and internal memos. ## `$CONCLUSION_REGISTRY` This contains accepted operating rules, each linked to evidence. # VII. Experiment system design Your production system should treat every campaign as an experimentable object. ```bash $EXPERIMENT_TYPES = { hook_test, caption_test, cta_test, sequencing_test, schedule_test, format_test, identity_coherence_test, platform_policy_test, repost_decay_test } ``` Example: ```yaml HookTest: treatment: "0-2 second hard visual interruption" control: "0-2 second context lead-in" primary_metric: "3s retention" secondary_metric: "follow conversion" stratification: ["platform","audience_band","asset_class"] ``` # VIII. Production metrics hierarchy Do not flatten everything into “engagement.” ```bash $METRIC_HIERARCHY = { attention_metrics, identity_metrics, audience_metrics, economic_metrics, platform_evolution_metrics } ``` ## `$ATTENTION_METRICS` On platforms where available, these should include retention-related measures, watch-time structures, and packaging effects. YouTube explicitly surfaces audience retention, average view duration, impressions CTR, and audience categories such as new, casual, and regular viewers. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$IDENTITY_METRICS` These are internal, not native platform metrics. ```bash $IDENTITY_METRICS = { natural_center_similarity, cross-post_symbolic_consistency, tonal_drift_score, aesthetic_signature_deviation } ``` ## `$AUDIENCE_METRICS` ```bash $AUDIENCE_METRICS = { new_viewer_rate, returning_viewer_rate, follower_conversion_rate, repeat_engagement_rate, lead_conversion_rate } ``` ## `$ECONOMIC_METRICS` ```bash $ECONOMIC_METRICS = { asset_yield_multiplier, cost_per_generated_unit, cost_per_follower, cost_per_lead, gross_asset_return } ``` ## `$PLATFORM_EVOLUTION_METRICS` ```bash $PLATFORM_EVOLUTION_METRICS = { strategy_decay_rate, platform_shift_alert_count, ranking_assumption_confidence, connector_capability_delta } ``` # IX. Workflow orchestration Use an event bus plus a workflow engine. ```bash $ARCHITECTURE_STYLE = "event-driven control plane + async workflow execution" ``` A minimal event vocabulary: ```text asset.registered asset.transcoded fragments.generated natural_center.computed platform_profile.updated units.generated schedule.computed publish.completed analytics.ingested experiment.started experiment.completed platform_change.detected policy.updated ``` # X. Infrastructure pattern Use containerized microservices, object storage, warehouse, queue, and workflow orchestrator. ```bash $INFRA = { compute: "Kubernetes or equivalent container orchestration", storage: "object store for media + warehouse for analytics", queue: "Kafka or equivalent event bus", workflow: "Temporal or equivalent durable workflow engine", cache: "Redis or equivalent", db: "Postgres for operational truth", observability: "OpenTelemetry + logs + traces + alerting" } ``` # XI. Environment-variable contract Per your standing preference, the system should be parameterized from the start. ```bash $APP_ENV $SYSTEM_ID $PUBLIC_APP_URI $GATEWAY_PORT $POSTGRES_URI $REDIS_URI $EVENT_BUS_URI $WORKFLOW_ENGINE_URI $OBJECT_STORE_URI $DATA_WAREHOUSE_URI $MODEL_REGISTRY_URI $EVIDENCE_STORE_URI $OPENAI_API_KEY $EMBEDDING_MODEL_ID $GENERATION_MODEL_ID $VISION_MODEL_ID $YOUTUBE_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $TIKTOK_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $LINKEDIN_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $INSTAGRAM_CONNECTOR_ENABLED $YOUTUBE_CLIENT_ID $YOUTUBE_CLIENT_SECRET $TIKTOK_CLIENT_ID $TIKTOK_CLIENT_SECRET $LINKEDIN_CLIENT_ID $LINKEDIN_CLIENT_SECRET $NATURAL_CENTER_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD $TONAL_DRIFT_THRESHOLD $EXPERIMENT_MIN_SAMPLE_SIZE $SIGNIFICANCE_ALPHA $PLATFORM_SHIFT_ALERT_THRESHOLD $ASSET_RETIREMENT_HALF_LIFE_DAYS ``` # XII. Deployment topology There are three correct environments. ```bash $DEPLOYMENT_STAGES = { research, staging, production } ``` ## `$RESEARCH` Loose, hypothesis-heavy, lower automation, high logging. ## `$STAGING` Connector-safe, sandbox posting where possible, simulated analytics replay. ## `$PRODUCTION` Version-locked connectors, auditable experiments, strict approval policies. # XIII. Human roles in the loop Production-grade does not mean human-free. ```bash $ROLES = { research_lead, platform_analyst, experiment_owner, brand_identity_editor The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.

Make sure to include fileciteturn1file2 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. Correct sequencing is: ```text dissertation-grade → operator-grade ``` Reason: ```text Operator systems without deep grounding → brittle, overfit, non-transferable Dissertation layer → defines invariants that operator layer executes against ``` So we proceed with: ```bash $PHASE = "DISERTATION_GRADE_RESEARCH_ATLAS_V1" ``` --- # I. ATLAS STRUCTURE ```bash $RESEARCH_ATLAS = { pillars: [...], per_pillar: { canonical_sources, core_concepts, temporal_analysis, system_mapping, research_questions } } ``` --- # II. PILLAR 1 — CAUSAL INFERENCE ## Canonical Sources ```text - Judea Pearl — Causality (2009) - Hernán & Robins — Causal Inference (2020) - Imbens & Rubin — Causal Inference for Statistics (2015) - Angrist & Pischke — Mostly Harmless Econometrics (2008) ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - counterfactuals - treatment vs control - confounding variables - causal graphs (DAGs) ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Econometrics + advertising experiments ### Present Gap Content systems lack causal isolation ### Future (System) ```text fragment-level causal attribution ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "Attribution Engine" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text Which content features causally drive follower conversion? ``` --- # III. PILLAR 2 — INFORMATION THEORY ## Canonical Sources ```text - Claude Shannon — Mathematical Theory of Communication (1948) - Cover & Thomas — Elements of Information Theory (2006) - MacKay — Information Theory, Inference, and Learning (2003) ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - entropy - signal vs noise - compression - channel capacity ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Communication systems optimization ### Present Gap Content treated as aesthetic, not signal ### Future ```text asset → maximum signal extraction → multi-channel transmission ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "Decomposition Engine" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text What is the maximum extractable signal per asset? ``` --- # IV. PILLAR 3 — ATTENTION & COGNITION ## Canonical Sources ```text - Kahneman — Attention and Effort (1973) - Kahneman — Thinking, Fast and Slow (2011) - Herbert Simon — Designing Organizations for Attention (1971) - Davenport & Beck — Attention Economy (2001) ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - limited attention - cognitive load - salience - fast vs slow processing ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Scarcity of attention recognized ### Present Gap Optimizing for clicks, not sustained attention ### Future ```text attention curve engineering per content unit ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "Attention Capture Engine" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text What patterns maximize retention beyond 3 seconds? ``` --- # V. PILLAR 4 — BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS ## Canonical Sources ```text - Thaler & Sunstein — Nudge (2008) - Cialdini — Influence (1984) - Ariely — Predictably Irrational (2008) ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - heuristics - bias - social proof - commitment loops ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Human irrationality modeled ### Present Gap Content ≠ behavior change ### Future ```text content as state-transition trigger ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "CTA + Conversion Engine" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text Which triggers move viewer → follower? ``` --- # VI. PILLAR 5 — SYSTEMS THEORY ## Canonical Sources ```text - Ludwig von Bertalanffy — General System Theory - Donella Meadows — Thinking in Systems - Norbert Wiener — Cybernetics ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - feedback loops - emergence - system boundaries ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Systems thinking formalized ### Present Gap Content systems are linear, not feedback-driven ### Future ```text self-optimizing content system ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "Feedback Loop + Optimization" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text How does the system self-correct over time? ``` --- # VII. Below is the **procedural specification** for: ```bash id="luwime" $SUBSYSTEM_NATURAL_CENTER ``` This is not conceptual. This is an **executable extraction + encoding + enforcement pipeline** that plugs directly into `Cronus-Metabolus`. --- # I. SYSTEM OBJECT ```bash id="b12wd8" $OBJECT_NATURAL_CENTER ``` ## Definition A **constraint-generating core representation** that governs all downstream content. --- ## Formal Structure ```yaml id="iz8scp" $NATURAL_CENTER: id: <string> thematic_core: <vector> aesthetic_signature: <vector> tonal_vector: <vector> narrative_bias: <vector> symbolic_markers: <set> negative_space: <set> # what is explicitly NOT allowed invariants: <ruleset> mutation_bounds: <constraints> ``` --- # II. PROCEDURAL PIPELINE ## STAGE 0 — INPUTS ```bash id="4h1jwp" $INPUT_CORPUS = { hero_assets, past content (optional), brand references, founder/voice text (optional) } ``` Minimum viable: **1 premium asset** --- # III. STAGE 1 — SIGNAL EXTRACTION ## $PROC_SIGNAL_EXTRACTION ### Goal Extract **non-obvious recurring patterns**. --- ## Step 1.1 — Visual Pattern Extraction ```bash id="5aquu3" $PROC_VISUAL_PARSE ``` Outputs: ```yaml id="23z2gk" visual_features: color_distribution motion_profile composition_patterns subject_focus pacing_signature ``` --- ## Step 1.2 — Audio / Language Extraction (if present) ```bash id="tpifda" $PROC_LANGUAGE_PARSE ``` Outputs: ```yaml id="62vdab" linguistic_features: sentence_length_distribution lexical density tone polarity rhetorical structures ``` --- ## Step 1.3 — Semantic Compression ```bash id="1uszxl" $PROC_SEMANTIC_CLUSTER ``` Method: - embed frames / text - cluster embeddings - extract dominant themes Output: ```yaml id="1uyoim" themes: - cluster_1 - cluster_2 - cluster_3 ``` --- # IV. STAGE 2 — CENTER DERIVATION ## $PROC_CENTER_SYNTHESIS ### Goal Collapse extracted signals into a **low-entropy core**. --- ## Step 2.1 — Thematic Core ```bash id="4qd3bf" $THEMATIC_CORE = top_k(themes, k=3–5) ``` Constraint: - must be stable across assets - must be compressible to minimal expression --- ## Step 2.2 — Aesthetic Signature ```bash id="us6jb5" $AESTHETIC_SIGNATURE = normalize( color + composition + motion ) ``` Example structure: ```yaml id="xygfqk" aesthetic_signature: palette: [dark_neutrals, metallic_accents] motion: [slow, deliberate] framing: [center-weighted, negative-space heavy] ``` --- ## Step 2.3 — Tonal Vector ```bash id="q207nq" $TONAL_VECTOR = embed(language_features) ``` Axes: - formal ↔ casual - minimal ↔ expressive - cold ↔ emotional --- ## Step 2.4 — Narrative Bias ```bash id="c64z8h" $NARRATIVE_BIAS = detect_patterns( transformation, tension, reveal, authority, aspiration ) ``` --- ## Step 2.5 — Symbolic Markers ```bash id="6dnyrs" $SYMBOLIC_MARKERS = extract_recurring_elements ``` Examples: - visual motifs - phrases - structural beats --- ## Step 2.6 — Negative Space (CRITICAL) ```bash id="qpx92j" $NEGATIVE_SPACE = detect_absences ``` This defines: ```text id="r5kuv4" what the system must NEVER produce ``` Example: - no humor - no fast cuts - no bright palettes --- # V. STAGE 3 — INVARIANT CONSTRUCTION ## $PROC_INVARIANT_BUILD ### Goal Convert center into **hard constraints** --- ## Rule Types ### 1. Structural Invariants ```yaml id="alza3w" rules: - "all outputs must reflect at least 1 thematic_core element" - "visual outputs must stay within palette bounds" ``` --- ### 2. Tonal Invariants ```yaml id="zcuv9u" rules: - "no slang" - "sentence length > threshold" ``` --- ### 3. Prohibited States ```yaml id="1pwkwn" rules: - "no meme formats" - "no generic stock tone" ``` --- # VI. STAGE 4 — ENCODING ## $PROC_CENTER_ENCODING ### Output Object ```json id="rol0b2" { "id": "nc_<hash>", "embedding": <vector>, "rules": <ruleset>, "signature": <compressed representation> } ``` Stored at: ```bash id="7ziyde" $NATURAL_CENTER_STORE_URI ``` --- # VII. STAGE 5 — ENFORCEMENT ## $PROC_CENTER_ENFORCEMENT This is where most systems fail. --- ## Injection Points ### 1. Content Generation ```bash id="aetwvj" generate(content | constraints = $NATURAL_CENTER) ``` --- ### 2. Caption Generation ```bash id="wzhiro" LLM(prompt + tonal_vector + invariants) ``` --- ### 3. Post Filtering ```bash id="9ab85f" if !validate(output, $NATURAL_CENTER): reject ``` --- ## Validation Function ```bash id="fquar5" $FUNC_VALIDATE(output): score = similarity(output_embedding, center_embedding) if score < threshold: return FAIL if violates_rules(output): return FAIL return PASS ``` --- # VIII. STAGE 6 — FEEDBACK LOOP ## $PROC_CENTER_EVOLUTION ### Inputs - high-performing outputs - low-performing outputs --- ## Update Logic ```bash id="geq20u" reinforce(features from high performers) suppress(features from low performers) ``` --- ## Constraint ```text id="befdr7" center must evolve slowly (low volatility) ``` --- # IX. FAILURE MODES ## 1. Over-generalization - center too broad → generic content ## 2. Over-constraining - center too narrow → no variation ## 3. Drift - feedback loop overrides identity --- # X. FULL PIPELINE INTEGRATION ```text id="2e4equ" [ASSET] → SIGNAL EXTRACTION → CENTER SYNTHESIS → NATURAL CENTER (stored) → Cronus-Metabolus → content generation (constrained) → validation layer → deployment → feedback → center evolution ``` --- # XI. MINIMAL IMPLEMENTATION (V1) If you were to implement immediately: ```bash id="mqug1p" $PIPELINE_V1: - CLIP extraction (ffmpeg + scene detect) - OPENAI embeddings for semantic clustering - LLM prompt template w/ tone constraints - simple rule-based validator ``` --- # XII. To prevent this, he **swallowed each child at birth**. ### Children involved The children he consumed were: - Hestia - Demeter - Hera - Hades - Poseidon His wife, **Rhea**, eventually intervened: - She hid their youngest child, **Zeus** - She gave Cronus a **stone wrapped in cloth**, which he swallowed instead ### Resolution When Zeus matured, he forced Cronus to **regurgitate his siblings**, and then led the Olympians in overthrowing him (the Titanomachy). ### Structural interpretation (useful for your system work) Cronus represents: - **Self-consuming systems** (systems that destroy their own outputs to preserve control) - **Fear-driven recursion failure** - **Temporal devouring** (time consuming its own productions — later philosophically mapped onto Saturn/Time) If you want, I can map Cronus into your myth→technology assignment framework (e.g., which system archetype or computational failure mode he encodes).

The source corpus explicitly defines the intended flow as asset ingestion, media analysis, Natural Center extraction, generation, publishing, analytics ingestion, attribution, experimentation, and platform adaptation. fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file1 fileciteturn2file2 ## Exact algorithmic frame The cleanest formalization is this: ```text content = f(asset, natural_center, platform_profile) success = f(content, platform_state(t), audience_state) ``` That is already implicit in your sources: the system is not a scheduler; it is an extraction-and-yield engine built around premium source material, Natural Center enforcement, and time-varying platform conditions. fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file1 I would define the system as seven strict algorithms: ```text ALG_01 = multimodal asset normalization ALG_02 = multimodal fragment extraction ALG_03 = natural center derivation ALG_04 = fragment scoring ALG_05 = platform-fit scoring ALG_06 = audience-transition scoring ALG_07 = post-publication attribution and learning ``` --- ## 1. ALG_01 — multimodal asset normalization Purpose: convert heterogeneous input into a unified analyzable representation. ### Accepted input classes Your sources already imply these classes: ```text video image_set / render_set audio text / brand voice corpus historical post corpus ``` fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file2 ### Canonical normalized object ```json { "asset_id": "A_*", "asset_type": "video|image_set|audio|document|mixed", "streams": { "video": [...], "audio": [...], "text": [...], "image": [...] }, "metadata": { "duration_ms": 0, "fps": 0, "resolution": [0, 0], "language": "en", "brand_id": "B_*" } } ``` ### Exact procedure ```text PROC_NORMALIZE(asset): 1. Compute checksum(asset) 2. Detect asset_type 3. If video: demux into video stream + audio stream transcode to canonical formats sample frames at adaptive interval 4. If image_set/render_set: normalize dimensions, color profile, EXIF 5. If audio: normalize sample rate and loudness 6. If text: clean, segment, and encode paragraphs/sentences 7. Persist all derivative streams 8. Emit asset.normalized ``` --- ## 2. ALG_02 — multimodal fragment extraction Purpose: produce candidate units from dense assets. Your sources already call for clip extraction at high-energy moments, scene detection, keyframe extraction, pacing analysis, and segmentation. fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file1 ### 2.1 Video fragment extraction For video, define fragments over a change-point lattice rather than fixed windows. ```text Let V = video timeline Let C = set of candidate cut points C = union( scene boundaries, shot boundaries, transcript punctuation peaks, silence boundaries, audio onset peaks, motion regime changes ) ``` Construct candidate intervals: ```text F_video = {(c_i, c_j) | min_len <= c_j - c_i <= max_len} ``` Recommended bounds: ```text min_len = 3s max_len = 45s platform-specific downstream pruning applies ``` ### 2.2 Image fragment extraction For renders and stills, fragments are not temporal; they are compositional derivatives. ```text F_image = union( full-frame, crop_subject, crop_detail, before_after_pair, comparison_layout, lifestyle_context_projection, carousel_sequence ) ``` ### 2.3 Audio fragment extraction ```text F_audio = union( speech segment, hook phrase, emphasis burst, music drop, silence-contrast interval ) ``` ### 2.4 Text fragment extraction ```text F_text = union( claim, hook line, authority line, transformation line, CTA line, FAQ line ) ``` ### 2.5 Exact multimodal candidate generation ```text PROC_EXTRACT_FRAGMENTS(asset): 1. Detect scene boundaries on video 2. Detect shot boundaries 3. ASR transcript with token timestamps 4. Compute audio envelope, spectral flux, silence regions 5. Compute motion magnitude and saliency over time 6. Merge all boundary proposals 7. Generate candidate windows 8. Generate image crops/layout variants 9. Generate text units from transcript and corpus 10. Return multimodal candidate set F ``` --- ## 3. ALG_03 — Natural Center derivation This is already partially specified in the source corpus: thematic core, aesthetic signature, tonal vector, narrative bias, symbolic markers, negative space, invariants, mutation bounds. fileciteturn2file2 ### Exact Natural Center object ```json { "nc_id": "NC_*", "thematic_core": [t1, t2, t3, t4, t5], "aesthetic_signature": { "palette": [...], "motion": [...], "composition": [...], "texture": [...] }, "tonal_vector": [formal, expressive, emotional, authoritative, playful], "narrative_bias": [transformation, tension, reveal, aspiration, proof], "symbolic_markers": [...], "negative_space": [...], "invariants": [...], "mutation_bounds": {...}, "embedding": [...] } ``` ### Exact derivation algorithm ```text PROC_DERIVE_NATURAL_CENTER(corpus): 1. For all visual assets: extract color histograms, embeddings, composition maps, motion stats 2. For all text/audio assets: extract sentence-length distribution, lexical density, rhetoric markers, semantic embeddings 3. Cluster semantic embeddings -> candidate themes 4. Select stable top-k themes across corpus 5. Compute aesthetic centroid across visual features 6. Compute tonal centroid across language features 7. Detect recurring symbolic elements 8. Detect systematic absences -> negative_space 9. Generate invariants from high-frequency stable features 10. Set mutation bounds from historical variance 11. Encode final Natural Center embedding ``` ### Stability rule A feature enters the Natural Center only if: ```text stability(feature) = presence_across_assets * consistency_across_assets accept iff stability(feature) >= τ_stability ``` --- ## 4. ALG_04 — fragment scoring This is the core extraction-scoring layer you asked for. Each candidate fragment gets a multimodal score vector, then a scalar aggregate. ### 4.1 Base feature vector per fragment For each fragment `f`, compute: ```text x(f) = [ visual_saliency, motion_energy, cut_density, subject_prominence, face_presence, product_presence, text_overlay_legibility, audio_energy, speech_clarity, hook_density, semantic_novelty, thematic_alignment, tonal_alignment, narrative_tension, symbolic_marker_density, platform_shape_fit ] ``` ### 4.2 Exact sub-scores #### Visual saliency ```text visual_saliency(f) = mean_t saliency_map_intensity(t) over fragment ``` #### Motion energy ```text motion_energy(f) = mean_t ||optical_flow(t)|| normalized by asset baseline ``` #### Cut density ```text cut_density(f) = number_of_shot_changes / fragment_duration_sec ``` #### Subject prominence ```text subject_prominence(f) = mean_t (largest_subject_bbox_area / frame_area) ``` #### Product presence ```text product_presence(f) = visible_time_of_primary_object / fragment_duration ``` #### Audio energy ```text audio_energy(f) = RMS(audio_signal_f) z-scored within asset ``` #### Speech clarity ```text speech_clarity(f) = 1 - WER_proxy(f) ``` Use ASR confidence as a proxy if needed. #### Hook density Define hooks as high-information lines or events in the first N seconds. ```text hook_density(f) = (count of hook tokens + count of high-saliency events in first 3s) / 3 ``` #### Semantic novelty ```text semantic_novelty(f) = distance(embedding(f), centroid(previous_selected_fragments)) ``` This prevents near-duplicate output. #### Thematic alignment ```text thematic_alignment(f) = max cosine_similarity(embedding(f), thematic_core_i) ``` #### Tonal alignment ```text tonal_alignment(f) = 1 - normalized_distance(tone_vector(f), tone_vector(NC)) ``` #### Narrative tension ```text narrative_tension(f) = weighted_sum( unresolved_question_presence, contrast_event_density, reveal_probability, expectation_violation ) ``` #### Symbolic marker density ```text symbolic_marker_density(f) = matched_symbolic_markers / possible_symbolic_markers ``` #### Platform shape fit This is structural, before any performance data. ```text platform_shape_fit(f,p) = g(duration_fit, aspect_ratio_fit, opening_velocity_fit, subtitle_density_fit) ``` ### 4.3 Hard constraint gating Before aggregate scoring, fail fragments that violate invariant rules: ```text if violates_negative_space(f, NC): reject if tonal_alignment(f) < τ_tone: reject if text_legibility(f,p) < τ_legibility: reject if safe_crop_confidence(f,p) < τ_crop: reject ``` ### 4.4 Aggregate fragment score ```text fragment_score(f,p) = w1*visual_saliency + w2*motion_energy + w3*subject_prominence + w4*audio_energy + w5*hook_density + w6*thematic_alignment + w7*tonal_alignment + w8*narrative_tension + w9*symbolic_marker_density + w10*semantic_novelty + w11*platform_shape_fit - w12*redundancy_penalty - w13*constraint_risk ``` Where: ```text redundancy_penalty = max similarity to already-selected fragments constraint_risk = predicted probability of violating NC or platform constraints ``` ### 4.5 Selection rule ```text select top-N fragments per platform subject to: pairwise similarity <= τ_dup thematic coverage >= τ_coverage duration bucket diversity narrative role diversity ``` This is better than naive top-N. --- ## 5. ALG_05 — platform-fit scoring Your sources explicitly require platform-aware generation and platform profiles with ranking features, format weights, timing bias, and time-varying platform state. fileciteturn2file1 ### Platform profile ```json { "platform": "tiktok", "format_weights": { "short_video": 0.8, "carousel": 0.1, "text": 0.1 }, "ranking_features": ["hook_retention", "replayability", "comment_rate"], "timing_bias": {...}, "constraints": {...}, "confidence_score": 0.82 } ``` ### Exact platform-fit score ```text platform_fit(f,p,t) = a1*duration_fit(f,p) + a2*opening_pattern_fit(f,p) + a3*caption_density_fit(f,p) + a4*loopability(f,p) + a5*replayability(f,p) + a6*format_weight_match(f,p,t) + a7*current_platform_preference_match(p,t) ``` ### Platform-specific heuristics ```text YouTube Shorts: emphasize clarity + retention + pay-off density TikTok: emphasize opening disruption + replayability + comment provocation LinkedIn: emphasize authority framing + slower pacing + insight density ``` That direction is consistent with the source set’s platform-profile logic. fileciteturn2file1 --- ## 6. ALG_06 — audience-transition scoring Your documents already move beyond engagement into audience-state transitions: viewer, engaged viewer, follower, returning follower, converted user, advocate. fileciteturn1file6 ### State machine ```text S0 unaware S1 viewer S2 engaged_viewer S3 follower S4 returning_follower S5 converted_user S6 advocate ``` ### Transition probabilities For each published unit `u`: ```text P(S2|S1,u) = sigmoid(β0 + β1*retention_3s + β2*completion_rate + β3*symbolic_marker_density + β4*hook_density) P(S3|S2,u) = sigmoid(γ0 + γ1*identity_binding + γ2*cta_strength + γ3*authority_signal + γ4*repeat_exposure_count) P(S4|S3,u) = sigmoid(δ0 + δ1*seriality + δ2*cadence_match + δ3*theme_continuity) P(S5|S4,u) = sigmoid(η0 + η1*offer_relevance + η2*trust_signal + η3*proof_density) ``` ### Identity binding score Since the sources insist that Natural Center persistence matters, define: ```text identity_binding(u) = b1*tonal_alignment + b2*symbolic_marker_density + b3*aesthetic_signature_match + b4*theme_continuity ``` This supports the “consistency -> recognition -> retention -> conversion” claim already present in the corpus. fileciteturn1file7 --- ## 7. ALG_07 — attribution and learning The sources already specify the required mapping: ```text performance -> publish_event -> unit -> fragment -> asset ``` and continuous experimentation with significance testing. fileciteturn2file1 ### Exact attribution chain ```text PROC_ATTRIBUTE(obs): 1. Resolve publish_id from platform event 2. Resolve unit_id from publish_id 3. Resolve fragment_id from unit_id 4. Resolve asset_id from fragment_id 5. Write observation to all four levels: unit fragment asset natural_center_version ``` ### Learning update ```text For each feature k: weight_k(t+1) = weight_k(t) + λ * contribution_k_to_success - μ * contribution_k_to_failure ``` Where success is not raw engagement only; it is a weighted portfolio: ```text success_score(u) = c1*attention_efficiency + c2*follow_conversion + c3*retention_ratio + c4*lead_conversion + c5*content_half_life ``` The source set already defines these downstream metrics directionally. fileciteturn1file6turn2file1 --- ## 8. Exact scoring stack This is the full stack I would use. ```text score_total(u,p,t) = α1 * score_fragment + α2 * score_natural_center_match + α3 * score_platform_fit + α4 * score_predicted_audience_transition + α5 * score_diversity_contribution + α6 * score_economic_yield_prediction - α7 * score_duplication_risk - α8 * score_constraint_violation_risk - α9 * score_platform_decay_risk ``` ### Definitions ```text score_fragment = intrinsic content strength score_natural_center_match = identity preservation score_platform_fit = structural compatibility with platform-state(t) score_predicted_audience_transition = probability of moving audience to next state score_diversity_contribution = usefulness relative to already-selected units score_economic_yield_prediction = expected follower/lead yield score_duplication_risk = near-duplicate or cannibalized output score_constraint_violation_risk = Natural Center or technical rule risk score_platform_decay_risk = overuse of patterns losing efficacy on current platform ``` --- ## 9.
