Do not include any extraneous text, backticks, markdown formatting, or comments beyond what is shown. - The `queries` field must be a list of strings. - One of the queries **MUST** exactly match the user's original question, but stripped of any extraneous details, and with ambiguous references clearly resolved using context from the conversation. It **MUST** be a complete sentence. - Even if you suspect the user meant something else, one query **MUST** still be their original question. ## How to handle results from file_search The file search tool will respond with the relevant search results from the full files. After you receive these results: - Carefully evaluate each result to determine its relevance and quality. - Use the high-quality and relevant results to inform your answer to the user's question. - When possible, reuse information from prior file_search results and avoid repeating identical queries. ## Tool usage instructions and ordering When the user asks a question, carefully consider whether the provided snippets sufficiently answer the query, whether you need to invoke `file_search.msearch`, or another available tool (such as `web`, if available) should be used instead. Tool-ordering Guidelines: 1. For queries related to uploaded files, if snippets are insufficient, strongly prefer invoking `file_search.msearch` over other tools. - Avoid using `web.run`, `python` analysis, or any other tools until you have either: - Confirmed with high confidence the answer is not in the files, or - Have high confidence `file_search_tool` queries are not sufficient to answer the query. 2. Avoid usage of `web_search/web.run` for queries related to uploaded files - Carefully consider whether the user's query is better answered by web search results rather than available snippets or results from `file_search_tool`. - Unless the user's query is clearly unrelated to the uploaded files, prefer using `file_search.msearch`. You can always follow up with a web search if necessary for a more complete response. 3. Avoid using `python` unless necessary or requested by user. - If the user's query is answerable from the parsed text and images from the uploaded files, or from `file_search_tool`, do not invoke `python`. Always keep this instruction in mind for the rest of the conversation, as the user may reference these files or ask related questions later on.

```bash $NEXT="NC_ENGINE_V2 + SCORING_UNIFICATION_LAYER" ``` This is the correct next artifact. It closes the remaining gap identified in the source set: **Natural Center exists as a computable object, but it is not yet fully closed under scoring, attribution, experimentation, and bounded evolution**. The missing move is to make `NC` the **single measurable identity field** that participates uniformly in extraction, generation, validation, scoring, attribution, and learning. fileciteturn4file19 fileciteturn4file16 --- # I. OBJECTIVE ```text Turn Natural Center from: constraint object into: stateful, measurable, versioned scoring engine + unified alignment layer across all operators ``` Formally, the target is: \[ NC\_Engine = \langle NC,\ BE,\ \Sigma,\ \mathcal{M},\ \mathcal{C},\ \mathcal{U} \rangle \] where: \[ NC = \text{symbolic Natural Center object} \] \[ BE = \text{brand embedding projection} \] \[ \Sigma = \text{scoring surface} \] \[ \mathcal{M} = \text{metric registry} \] \[ \mathcal{C} = \text{causal hooks} \] \[ \mathcal{U} = \text{bounded update rule} \] This is exactly the expansion the computability review says is missing: scoring function, metric surface, causal participation, and controlled update rule. fileciteturn4file19 --- # II. CORE THESIS The system currently has: ```text NC → generation constraints BE → partial vector interface fragment scoring → separate attribution → separate audience transitions → separate ``` It needs: ```text one unified scoring layer ``` So define: \[ \mathcal{S}_{unified}(x,p,t) = \alpha \cdot Intrinsic(x) + \beta \cdot NCAlign(x) + \gamma \cdot PlatformFit(x,p,t) + \delta \cdot AudienceFit(x) - \eta \cdot Risk(x) \] This becomes the common function family used at different points in the stack. --- # III. NC_ENGINE_V2 ```bash $NC_ENGINE_V2 ``` ## III.1 Canonical Object ```yaml NaturalCenterV2: nc_id: string tenant_id: string workspace_id: string brand_id: string version: int thematic_core: list aesthetic_signature: dict tonal_vector: dict narrative_bias: dict symbolic_markers: list negative_space: list invariants: list mutation_bounds: dict embedding: semantic_vector: vector visual_vector: vector tonal_vector: vector narrative_vector: vector symbolic_vector: vector negative_vector: vector fused_vector: vector metrics: nc_coherence: float nc_consistency: float nc_drift: float nc_recognition: float | null confidence_score: float review_status: draft|reviewed|approved effective_from: timestamp effective_to: timestamp|null ``` This merges the symbolic Natural Center structure with the multi-vector Brand Embedding structure and elevates NC metrics to first-class fields. fileciteturn4file16 fileciteturn4file17 fileciteturn4file19 --- ## III.2 Required APIs ```python def derive_natural_center_v2(corpus: list[Asset]) -> NaturalCenterV2 def encode_natural_center_v2(nc: NaturalCenterV2) -> BrandEmbedding def nc_score(output: GeneratedUnit, nc: NaturalCenterV2) -> float def nc_metric_surface(outputs: list[GeneratedUnit], nc: NaturalCenterV2) -> dict def update_natural_center_v2(nc: NaturalCenterV2, evidence: EvidenceBundle) -> NaturalCenterV2 ``` --- ## III.3 Required Internal Modules ```text nc-bootstrap nc-encoder nc-validator nc-metrics nc-causal nc-evolution ``` --- # IV. SCORING UNIFICATION LAYER ```bash $SCORING_UNIFICATION_LAYER ``` This is the real system closure. Every score in the system must become a specialization of one master interface: ```python def unified_score( candidate, nc: NaturalCenterV2, platform_profile: PlatformProfile, audience_context: AudienceContext, scoring_mode: str, ) -> ScoreBundle ``` Return: ```yaml ScoreBundle: total_score: float intrinsic_score: float nc_alignment_score: float platform_fit_score: float audience_fit_score: float risk_penalty: float component_scores: dict passed_hard_constraints: bool score_version: string ``` --- # V. UNIFIED SCORE FAMILY ## V.1 Master Equation \[ Score(x,p,a,t) = w_I I(x) + w_N N(x,NC) + w_P P(x,p,t) + w_A A(x,a) - w_R R(x) \] where: \[ I(x) = \text{intrinsic content quality} \] \[ N(x,NC) = \text{Natural Center alignment} \] \[ P(x,p,t) = \text{platform fit under } platform\_state(t) \] \[ A(x,a) = \text{audience-transition fitness} \] \[ R(x) = \text{penalty surface} \] This replaces the current fragmented logic with one compositional scoring doctrine. It is consistent with the formal operator model and the algorithmic frame already defined in the source set. fileciteturn4file23 fileciteturn4file13 --- ## V.2 Specializations ### Fragment selection score \[ Score_{fragment}(f) = w_I I(f) + w_N N(f,NC) + w_P P(f,p,t) - w_R R(f) \] ### Generated unit validation score \[ Score_{unit}(u) = w_N N(u,NC) + w_P P(u,p,t) + w_A A(u,a) - w_R R(u) \] ### Attribution identity modifier \[ IdentityAdj(u) = N(u,NC) \] ### Learning update acceptance score \[ Score_{update} = w_C CausalLift + w_S Stability - w_D DriftRisk \] --- # VI. NC ALIGNMENT SURFACE This is the most important addition. Natural Center alignment cannot remain a single opaque cosine score. It must be decomposed. Define: \[ N(x,NC)= \lambda_T T_{align} +\lambda_A A_{align} +\lambda_{\Tau} \Tau_{align} +\lambda_N N_{align} +\lambda_S S_{align} -\lambda_{\Omega}\Omega_{viol} \] where: ```text T_align = thematic alignment A_align = aesthetic alignment Tau_align = tonal alignment N_align = narrative alignment S_align = symbolic marker density / recurrence Omega_viol = negative-space violation score ``` This directly operationalizes the full NC object rather than reducing it to a flat embedding comparison. fileciteturn4file16 fileciteturn4file19 ### Practical V1 formula ```python def nc_score(output, nc): return clamp( 0.22 * thematic_alignment(output, nc) + 0.18 * aesthetic_alignment(output, nc) + 0.18 * tonal_alignment(output, nc) + 0.14 * narrative_alignment(output, nc) + 0.18 * symbolic_alignment(output, nc) - 0.20 * negative_space_violation(output, nc), 0.0, 1.0, ) ``` --- # VII. METRIC SURFACE The source set explicitly says NC must be independently measurable, not merely used as a modifier. fileciteturn4file19 So define four first-class metrics. ## VII.1 NC Coherence Per output: \[ NC\_coherence(u)=N(u,NC) \] Meaning: how aligned one output is to the current Natural Center. ## VII.2 NC Consistency Per campaign: \[ NC\_consistency(C)=1-\frac{1}{|C|}\sum_{u \in C} D(u,\mu_C) \] Meaning: how mutually coherent outputs are with each other under the same center. ## VII.3 NC Drift Across time: \[ NC\_drift(t_1,t_2)=D(NC_{t_1},NC_{t_2}) \] Where \(D\) is the mixed symbolic-vector distance proposed in the proof bridge. fileciteturn4file24 ## VII.4 NC Recognition Audience-facing, later-stage metric: \[ NC\_recognition = f(recall,\ repeat\_engagement,\ branded\_search,\ symbolic\_association) \] This can start as null or experimental until instrumentation matures. --- # VIII. CAUSAL HOOKS Natural Center must enter the experimentation layer directly, not implicitly. fileciteturn4file19 Define causal hypotheses such as: ```text HYP_NC_001: higher nc_coherence → higher retention_3s HYP_NC_002: higher symbolic_alignment → higher follow_conversion_rate HYP_NC_003: lower tonal_drift → higher returning_viewer_ratio ``` Formal lift: \[ Lift_{NC} = E[Y \mid high\_NC\_alignment] - E[Y \mid low\_NC\_alignment] \] Acceptance rule: ```python if p_value < ALPHA and lift_nc > 0: mark_signal_as_causally_validated() ``` This is the exact missing causal participation requirement. fileciteturn4file19 fileciteturn4file24 --- # IX. BOUNDED EVOLUTION RULE NC must evolve, but only under control. The source set is explicit on slow evolution and bounded drift. fileciteturn4file16 fileciteturn4file19 Define: \[ NC_{t+1} = NC_t + \Pi_{\epsilon}\bigl(\Delta_{validated}\bigr) \] where \(\Pi_{\epsilon}\) is a projection onto the bounded-drift ball. Operationally: ```python def update_natural_center_v2(nc_t, validated_signals, epsilon): candidate = apply_validated_updates(nc_t, validated_signals) if distance(candidate, nc_t) > epsilon: candidate = project_back(candidate, nc_t, epsilon) return candidate ``` Constraints: ```text 1. only causally validated signals may update NC 2. update must stay within mutation_bounds 3. negative_space cannot be weakened without review 4. symbolic markers require stronger evidence than hook patterns ``` --- # X. SERVICE MAP This artifact affects multiple services, not only `natural-center`. ## services/natural-center Owns: ```text derivation validation metric computation bounded evolution ``` ## services/brand-embedding Owns: ```text encode/decode vector storage similarity operations drift distance ``` ## services/media-analysis Owns: ```text intrinsic features fragment features candidate feature vectors ``` ## services/generation-orchestrator Consumes: ```text nc_score hard constraints platform-adjusted unified score ``` ## services/attribution-engine Consumes: ```text identity-adjusted value nc_coherence nc_consistency lift_nc ``` ## services/experimentation-engine Consumes: ```text nc-based hypotheses significance tests update admission decisions ``` This service split is fully consistent with the operator-grade and monorepo architecture already established. fileciteturn4file1 fileciteturn4file21 --- # XI. DATABASE / SCHEMA DELTA Additions to `natural_centers`: ```sql ALTER TABLE natural_centers ADD COLUMN confidence_score FLOAT NOT NULL, ADD COLUMN nc_coherence_baseline FLOAT NULL, ADD COLUMN nc_consistency_baseline FLOAT NULL, ADD COLUMN review_status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft', ADD COLUMN effective_from TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, ADD COLUMN effective_to TIMESTAMP NULL; ``` Create metric table: ```sql CREATE TABLE nc_metrics ( metric_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, nc_id TEXT NOT NULL, unit_id TEXT NULL, campaign_id TEXT NULL, metric_type TEXT NOT NULL, metric_value FLOAT NOT NULL, measured_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL ); ``` Create update log: ```sql CREATE TABLE nc_update_events ( update_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, nc_id TEXT NOT NULL, prior_version INT NOT NULL, next_version INT NOT NULL, evidence_bundle_uri TEXT NOT NULL, causal_validation_status TEXT NOT NULL, drift_distance FLOAT NOT NULL, approved_by TEXT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL ); ``` Add score versioning: ```sql ALTER TABLE generated_units ADD COLUMN scoring_version TEXT NOT NULL, ADD COLUMN unified_score FLOAT NULL, ADD COLUMN identity_adj_value FLOAT NULL; ``` --- # XII. WORKFLOW CHANGES ## Asset pipeline upgrade ```text asset.normalized → fragments.extracted → NC.bootstrap_or_load → BE.encoded → candidates.unified_scored → units.generated → units.identity_validated → publish.plan.created ``` ## Analytics pipeline upgrade ```text analytics.ingested → attribution.computed → nc_metrics.computed → causal_tests.updated → nc_update_candidate.evaluated ``` ## Review workflow ```text nc_update_candidate.created → causal_validation.checked → drift_bound.checked → human_review_if_required → nc_version.promoted ``` These changes align with the orchestration-first architecture and the requirement that research become runtime-bound, not passive. fileciteturn4file21 fileciteturn4file24 --- # XIII. TEST REGISTRY ## Contract tests ```text test_nc_score_in_range test_negative_space_blocks_invalid_output test_unified_score_is_deterministic test_update_requires_causal_validation test_update_respects_drift_bound ``` ## Integration tests ```text test_asset_to_nc_to_scored_fragments test_generated_unit_receives_unified_score test_attribution_uses_identity_adjustment test_experiment_can_measure_lift_nc ``` ## Statistical tests ```text test_high_nc_alignment_improves_retention test_symbolic_alignment_improves_follow_conversion test_tonal_drift_reduces_return_rate ``` --- # XIV. HARD GOVERNANCE RULES ```text no output may ship without nc_score no attribution may omit identity adjustment no nc update may occur without causal evidence no scoring function may bypass negative space no embedding update may exceed ε drift bound ``` --- # XV. FINAL FORM After this artifact, Natural Center becomes: ```text not just a stored object not just a generation constraint not just an embedding seed but: a versioned, measurable, causal, bounded identity field ``` That is the exact closure the source set has been pushing toward. fileciteturn4file19 fileciteturn4file17 fileciteturn4file24 --- # XVI. STRICT NEXT ARTIFACT ```bash $NEXT="NC_SCORE_API + UNIFIED_SCORE_SPEC + NC_METRIC_REGISTRY" ``` That would be the code-facing deliverable: exact formulas, typed schemas, thresholds, and function signatures.

## Assistant

```bash $NEXT="NC_SCORE_API + UNIFIED_SCORE_SPEC + NC_METRIC_REGISTRY" ``` This is the code-facing closure layer. It operationalizes three things that the source set has been demanding but not yet fully pinned down: 1. `NaturalCenter` must become a **first-class measurable operator**, not only a stored constraint object. fileciteturn5file19 fileciteturn5file16 2. all scoring surfaces must collapse into **one composable scoring doctrine** spanning fragment selection, generation validation, attribution adjustment, and bounded learning. fileciteturn5file26 fileciteturn5file13 3. the system must expose explicit, versioned **NC metrics** so identity can be evaluated, not merely enforced. fileciteturn5file19 --- # I. NC_SCORE_API ```bash $NC_SCORE_API_V1 ``` ## I.1 Purpose `NC_score` is the canonical identity-alignment function. It must be used at four points: ```text fragment scoring generated-unit validation attribution identity adjustment learning update admission ``` That requirement follows directly from the source diagnosis that Natural Center is not yet fully closed under scoring, attribution, experimentation, and evolution. fileciteturn5file19 fileciteturn5file26 --- ## I.2 Primary signature ```python def nc_score( candidate: "ScorableCandidate", nc: "NaturalCenterV2", mode: str = "unit", ) -> "NCScoreResult": ... ``` Allowed `mode` values: ```text fragment unit campaign update_candidate ``` --- ## I.3 Input contracts ```python class ScorableCandidate(TypedDict): candidate_id: str candidate_type: str semantic_features: dict visual_features: dict tonal_features: dict narrative_features: dict symbolic_features: dict negative_space_features: dict embedding: list[float] | None metadata: dict ``` ```python class NaturalCenterV2(TypedDict): nc_id: str version: int thematic_core: list[str] aesthetic_signature: dict tonal_vector: dict narrative_bias: dict symbolic_markers: list[str] negative_space: list[str] invariants: list[str] mutation_bounds: dict embedding: dict confidence_score: float ``` This structure is the direct continuation of the Natural Center object and Brand Embedding object already established in the source corpus. fileciteturn5file16 fileciteturn5file17 --- ## I.4 Output contract ```python class NCScoreResult(TypedDict): total_score: float thematic_alignment: float aesthetic_alignment: float tonal_alignment: float narrative_alignment: float symbolic_alignment: float negative_space_violation: float invariant_pass: bool hard_fail_reasons: list[str] score_version: str nc_id: str nc_version: int ``` --- ## I.5 Canonical formula \[ NCScore(x,NC)= \lambda_T T_{align} +\lambda_A A_{align} +\lambda_{\Tau}\Tau_{align} +\lambda_N N_{align} +\lambda_S S_{align} -\lambda_{\Omega}\Omega_{viol} \] Recommended V1 weights: ```python NC_SCORE_WEIGHTS = { "thematic": 0.22, "aesthetic": 0.18, "tonal": 0.18, "narrative": 0.14, "symbolic": 0.18, "negative": 0.20, } ``` This decomposition is required because NC alignment cannot remain a single opaque cosine score. fileciteturn5file26 --- ## I.6 Reference implementation ```python from typing import TypedDict, List, Dict class NCScoreResult(TypedDict): total_score: float thematic_alignment: float aesthetic_alignment: float tonal_alignment: float narrative_alignment: float symbolic_alignment: float negative_space_violation: float invariant_pass: bool hard_fail_reasons: List[str] score_version: str nc_id: str nc_version: int def _clamp(value: float, lo: float = 0.0, hi: float = 1.0) -> float: return max(lo, min(hi, value)) def nc_score(candidate: dict, nc: dict, mode: str = "unit") -> NCScoreResult: thematic_alignment = _clamp(candidate["semantic_features"]["thematic_alignment"]) aesthetic_alignment = _clamp(candidate["visual_features"]["aesthetic_alignment"]) tonal_alignment = _clamp(candidate["tonal_features"]["tonal_alignment"]) narrative_alignment = _clamp(candidate["narrative_features"]["narrative_alignment"]) symbolic_alignment = _clamp(candidate["symbolic_features"]["symbolic_alignment"]) negative_space_violation = _clamp( candidate["negative_space_features"]["negative_space_violation"] ) hard_fail_reasons: List[str] = [] if tonal_alignment < nc["mutation_bounds"].get("min_tonal_alignment", 0.60): hard_fail_reasons.append("tonal_alignment_below_floor") if negative_space_violation > nc["mutation_bounds"].get("max_negative_violation", 0.20): hard_fail_reasons.append("negative_space_violation") invariant_pass = len(hard_fail_reasons) == 0 total_score = _clamp( 0.22 * thematic_alignment + 0.18 * aesthetic_alignment + 0.18 * tonal_alignment + 0.14 * narrative_alignment + 0.18 * symbolic_alignment - 0.20 * negative_space_violation ) return NCScoreResult( total_score=total_score, thematic_alignment=thematic_alignment, aesthetic_alignment=aesthetic_alignment, tonal_alignment=tonal_alignment, narrative_alignment=narrative_alignment, symbolic_alignment=symbolic_alignment, negative_space_violation=negative_space_violation, invariant_pass=invariant_pass, hard_fail_reasons=hard_fail_reasons, score_version="nc_score_v1", nc_id=nc["nc_id"], nc_version=nc["version"], ) ``` --- ## I.7 Enforcement thresholds ```python NC_SCORE_THRESHOLDS = { "fragment_min": 0.62, "unit_min": 0.72, "campaign_mean_min": 0.75, "attribution_discount_floor": 0.55, "hard_reject_floor": 0.45, } ``` Meaning: ```text score < 0.45 → reject 0.45–0.55 → allow only in review mode 0.55–0.72 → can exist as candidate, not publishable ≥ 0.72 → publishable, subject to platform and audience checks ``` These thresholds are consistent with the bootstrap and validation logic already proposed in the corpus. fileciteturn5file20 --- # II. UNIFIED_SCORE_SPEC ```bash $UNIFIED_SCORE_SPEC_V1 ``` ## II.1 Purpose All scoring in the system must become a specialization of one function family. Without this, the system remains fragmented: ```text fragment scoring brand scoring platform scoring audience scoring attribution adjustment ``` With this, it becomes closed under one common doctrine. fileciteturn5file26 --- ## II.2 Master signature ```python def unified_score( candidate: "ScorableCandidate", nc: "NaturalCenterV2", platform_profile: "PlatformProfile", audience_context: "AudienceContext", scoring_mode: str, ) -> "UnifiedScoreResult": ... ``` Allowed `scoring_mode` values: ```text fragment_selection unit_validation publish_ranking attribution_identity_adjustment update_admission ``` --- ## II.3 Output contract ```python class UnifiedScoreResult(TypedDict): total_score: float intrinsic_score: float nc_alignment_score: float platform_fit_score: float audience_fit_score: float risk_penalty: float hard_constraint_pass: bool hard_fail_reasons: list[str] score_version: str mode: str ``` --- ## II.4 Master equation \[ Score(x,p,a,t)= w_I I(x) + w_N N(x,NC) + w_P P(x,p,t) + w_A A(x,a) - w_R R(x) \] where: ```text I = intrinsic quality score N = NC alignment score P = platform-fit score A = audience-fit score R = risk / penalty score ``` This is the exact scoring unification the previous artifact called for. fileciteturn5file26 --- ## II.5 Component definitions ### Intrinsic score Used mainly for fragment and unit quality. \[ I(x)= \sum_i \omega_i x_i \] Typical inputs come from the extraction stack: ```text visual_saliency motion_energy hook_density speech_clarity semantic_novelty subject_prominence ``` These are already specified in the algorithmic frame. fileciteturn5file13 ### NC alignment score \[ N(x,NC)=NCScore(x,NC) \] ### Platform fit score \[ P(x,p,t)= a_1 duration\_fit +a_2 opening\_pattern\_fit +a_3 caption\_density\_fit +a_4 format\_weight\_match +a_5 current\_platform\_preference\_match \] This follows the platform profile doctrine and platform-state model. fileciteturn5file1 fileciteturn5file13 ### Audience fit score \[ A(x,a)= b_1 retention\_potential +b_2 identity\_binding +b_3 CTA\_fitness +b_4 seriality\_support \] This is justified by the audience-state machine and audience engine layer. fileciteturn5file9 ### Risk penalty \[ R(x)= r_1 negative\_space\_violation +r_2 redundancy +r_3 crop\_risk +r_4 legibility\_risk +r_5 drift\_risk \] --- ## II.6 Mode-specific weights ```python UNIFIED_SCORE_MODE_WEIGHTS = { "fragment_selection": { "intrinsic": 0.38, "nc": 0.27, "platform": 0.25, "audience": 0.00, "risk": 0.10, }, "unit_validation": { "intrinsic": 0.10, "nc": 0.45, "platform": 0.25, "audience": 0.10, "risk": 0.10, }, "publish_ranking": { "intrinsic": 0.12, "nc": 0.28, "platform": 0.30, "audience": 0.20, "risk": 0.10, }, "attribution_identity_adjustment": { "intrinsic": 0.00, "nc": 0.75, "platform": 0.00, "audience": 0.00, "risk": 0.25, }, "update_admission": { "intrinsic": 0.00, "nc": 0.20, "platform": 0.00, "audience": 0.00, "risk": 0.80, }, } ``` --- ## II.7 Reference implementation ```python def unified_score(candidate, nc, platform_profile, audience_context, scoring_mode): nc_result = nc_score(candidate, nc, mode="unit") intrinsic_score = candidate["metadata"].get("intrinsic_score", 0.0) platform_fit_score = candidate["metadata"].get("platform_fit_score", 0.0) audience_fit_score = candidate["metadata"].get("audience_fit_score", 0.0) risk_penalty = candidate["metadata"].get("risk_penalty", 0.0) weights = UNIFIED_SCORE_MODE_WEIGHTS[scoring_mode] total_score = max( 0.0, min( 1.0, weights["intrinsic"] * intrinsic_score + weights["nc"] * nc_result["total_score"] + weights["platform"] * platform_fit_score + weights["audience"] * audience_fit_score - weights["risk"] * risk_penalty, ), ) hard_fail_reasons = list(nc_result["hard_fail_reasons"]) hard_constraint_pass = len(hard_fail_reasons) == 0 return UnifiedScoreResult( total_score=total_score, intrinsic_score=intrinsic_score, nc_alignment_score=nc_result["total_score"], platform_fit_score=platform_fit_score, audience_fit_score=audience_fit_score, risk_penalty=risk_penalty, hard_constraint_pass=hard_constraint_pass, hard_fail_reasons=hard_fail_reasons, score_version="unified_score_v1", mode=scoring_mode, ) ``` --- ## II.8 Hard constraints These are not soft penalties. They are gates. ```text negative-space violation above threshold tonal alignment below floor platform profile expired text legibility below floor safe crop confidence below floor ``` If any fail: ```text hard_constraint_pass = false ``` This remains consistent with the proof bridge and axiom registry: no score can bypass a hard invariant. fileciteturn5file24 fileciteturn5file25 --- # III. NC_METRIC_REGISTRY ```bash $NC_METRIC_REGISTRY_V1 ``` ## III.1 Purpose Natural Center must be independently measurable. The source corpus explicitly identifies this as missing. Identity cannot remain “enforced but unevaluated.” fileciteturn5file19 --- ## III.2 Canonical metrics ### NC_COHERENCE Per output: \[ NC\_COHERENCE(u)=NCScore(u,NC) \] Definition: ```text how aligned one output is to the active Natural Center ``` ### NC_CONSISTENCY Per campaign: \[ NC\_CONSISTENCY(C)=1-\frac{1}{|C|}\sum_{u\in C}D(u,\mu_C) \] Definition: ```text how internally coherent a set of outputs is ``` ### NC_DRIFT Across time: \[ NC\_DRIFT(NC_t,NC_{t+1})=D(NC_t,NC_{t+1}) \] Definition: ```text how much the active Natural Center changed between versions ``` ### NC_RECOGNITION Audience-facing: \[ NC\_RECOGNITION=f(repeat\_engagement, recall, branded\_search, symbolic\_association) \] Definition: ```text whether the audience is actually recognizing the identity field ``` ### NC_SYMBOLIC_DENSITY Per output: \[ NC\_SYMBOLIC\_DENSITY(u)=\frac{matched\_markers}{possible\_markers} \] ### NC_NEGATIVE_SPACE_RISK Per output: \[ NC\_NEGATIVE\_SPACE\_RISK(u)=\Omega_{viol} \] ### NC_TONAL_DRIFT Per output: \[ NC\_TONAL\_DRIFT(u)=1-\Tau_{align} \] --- ## III.3 Registry schema ```yaml NCMetricDefinition: metric_key: string metric_name: string level: output|campaign|nc_version|audience formula: string value_range: [float, float] direction: higher_is_better|lower_is_better threshold_warning: float | null threshold_fail: float | null required_inputs: list[str] owner_service: string version: string ``` --- ## III.4 V1 registry entries ```yaml - metric_key: nc_coherence metric_name: NC Coherence level: output formula: "NCScore(u, NC)" value_range: [0.0, 1.0] direction: higher_is_better threshold_warning: 0.72 threshold_fail: 0.45 required_inputs: [semantic_features, visual_features, tonal_features, narrative_features, symbolic_features, negative_space_features] owner_service: natural-center version: v1 - metric_key: nc_consistency metric_name: NC Consistency level: campaign formula: "1 - mean(distance(u_i, campaign_centroid))" value_range: [0.0, 1.0] direction: higher_is_better threshold_warning: 0.70 threshold_fail: 0.55 required_inputs: [campaign_output_embeddings] owner_service: natural-center version: v1 - metric_key: nc_drift metric_name: NC Drift level: nc_version formula: "distance(NC_t, NC_t_plus_1)" value_range: [0.0, 1.0] direction: lower_is_better threshold_warning: 0.12 threshold_fail: 0.20 required_inputs: [prior_nc, next_nc] owner_service: brand-embedding version: v1 - metric_key: nc_recognition metric_name: NC Recognition level: audience formula: "f(recall, repeat_engagement, branded_search, symbolic_association)" value_range: [0.0, 1.0] direction: higher_is_better threshold_warning: null threshold_fail: null required_inputs: [audience_signals] owner_service: audience-engine version: v1 ``` --- ## III.5 Storage schema ```sql CREATE TABLE nc_metric_definitions ( metric_key TEXT PRIMARY KEY, metric_name TEXT NOT NULL, level TEXT NOT NULL, formula TEXT NOT NULL, range_min FLOAT NOT NULL, range_max FLOAT NOT NULL, direction TEXT NOT NULL, threshold_warning FLOAT NULL, threshold_fail FLOAT NULL, required_inputs JSONB NOT NULL, owner_service TEXT NOT NULL, version TEXT NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW() ); CREATE TABLE nc_metric_observations ( observation_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, metric_key TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES nc_metric_definitions(metric_key), tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL, workspace_id TEXT NOT NULL, brand_id TEXT NOT NULL, nc_id TEXT NOT NULL, nc_version INT NOT NULL, unit_id TEXT NULL, campaign_id TEXT NULL, publish_id TEXT NULL, metric_value FLOAT NOT NULL, measured_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, metadata JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb ); ``` This schema extends the runtime research-and-operator model already required by the monorepo architecture. fileciteturn5file21 --- # IV. INTEGRATION RULES ## IV.1 Generation No generated unit may advance unless: ```text nc_score >= unit_min hard_constraint_pass = true ``` ## IV.2 Attribution Identity adjustment becomes: \[ IdentityAdj(u)=NC\_COHERENCE(u) \] Then: \[ V_p^{NC}=V_p^* \cdot IdentityAdj(u) \] This is the exact attribution integration called for in the attribution framework. fileciteturn5file14 ## IV.3 Experimentation Causal NC hypotheses must use registry metrics directly: ```text higher nc_coherence → higher retention_3s higher symbolic_density → higher follow_conversion lower tonal_drift → higher return rate ``` ## IV.4 Evolution No NC update may promote unless: ```text lift_nc > 0 nc_drift <= epsilon review_status = approved OR auto_promote_policy = true ``` This matches the bounded-evolution doctrine from the proof bridge and computability review. fileciteturn5file24 fileciteturn5file19 --- # V. TEST REGISTRY ```text test_nc_score_range_is_zero_to_one test_nc_score_rejects_negative_space_violation test_unified_score_is_deterministic_given_fixed_inputs test_unified_score_mode_weights_sum_correctly test_nc_metric_registry_contains_required_keys test_nc_coherence_written_for_every_publishable_unit test_attribution_uses_identity_adjustment test_nc_update_blocked_when_drift_exceeds_bound ``` --- # VI. GOVERNANCE ```text no publishable object without nc_coherence no score without score_version no metric without registry definition no attribution without identity adjustment no nc promotion without drift and causal checks ``` --- # VII. FINAL RESULT After this artifact, you have: ```text a formal NC scoring API a single unified score doctrine a first-class NC metric registry ``` That is the exact code-facing layer required to move from “Natural Center exists” to “Natural Center governs the system as a measurable computational field.” fileciteturn5file19 fileciteturn5file26 fileciteturn5file21 ```bash $NEXT="IMPLEMENTATION_FILES_V1" ``` Specifically: ```text packages/natural-center-sdk/src/nc_score.py packages/metric-definitions/src/unified_score.py data/seeds/metric-catalog/nc_metrics_v1.yaml contracts/metrics/nc_metric_registry.schema.json ```

## Assistant (artifact)

Make sure to include fileciteturn5file1 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. Yes. The operator layer should now be built as a **production research-and-deployment system**, not a mere posting tool. And your platform-evolution requirement is correct: the system must treat platform conditions as a **time-varying environment**, because the success function on YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, and LinkedIn is not stable across eras. Official sources already expose some of the observable surfaces you need to model: YouTube provides channel- and video-level analytics such as audience retention, impressions CTR, watch time, and new/casual/regular viewer segmentation; TikTok exposes posting workflows and developer constraints through its Content Posting APIs and guidelines; LinkedIn exposes organic posting primitives and some company-level engagement data via its marketing APIs. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) # $OPERATOR_GRADE_SYSTEM = "CRONUS_METABOLUS_OS" ```bash $MODE = "full-stack system (multi-service, production-grade)" $SYSTEM_NAME = "Cronus-Metabolus" $SYSTEM_CLASS = "Temporal Content Yield + Audience Formation Operating System" $PRIMARY_GOAL = "Convert premium assets into compounding audience, attribution, and platform-adaptive growth" $SECONDARY_GOAL = "Continuously research, detect, and adapt to platform-state changes over time" ``` # I. What this operator layer actually is This layer is the executable counterpart to the dissertation layer. The dissertation layer defines the invariants, causal claims, theoretical mappings, and research questions. The operator layer turns those into a running system that does six things at once: ingest assets, generate outputs, distribute them, measure results, run experiments, and update its understanding of both audience behavior and platform change. Formally: ```text Asset → Natural Center Extraction → Platform-Aware Generation → Scheduling + Distribution → Analytics + Attribution → Experimentation + Causal Analysis → Platform Change Detection → Model / Rule Updates → Repeat ``` # II. Production-grade service map The correct architecture is multi-service. Monolith first would be faster, but it will fail the research requirement, because research observability, platform adapters, experimentation, and identity enforcement need separate contracts. ```bash $SERVICES = { gateway_api, auth_identity, asset_ingestion, media_analysis, natural_center, generation_orchestrator, platform_intelligence, scheduling_engine, publishing_connectors, analytics_ingestion, attribution_engine, experimentation_engine, research_registry, reporting_dashboard, model_registry, event_bus, data_warehouse, object_storage, workflow_orchestrator, alerting_observability } ``` ## 1. `$SERVICE_GATEWAY_API` This is the system edge. It accepts user, client, asset, campaign, and experiment requests. It should expose stable contracts to UI, internal operators, and later partner integrations. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Entry point for all control-plane requests" $OUTPUTS = "campaign creation, asset registration, experiment submission, report fetch" ``` ## 2. `$SERVICE_ASSET_INGESTION` This service normalizes source material. It accepts hero films, render sets, stills, transcripts, briefing docs, and brand corpus. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "File intake, metadata extraction, transcode initiation, checksum, lineage" ``` ## 3. `$SERVICE_MEDIA_ANALYSIS` This is the decomposition engine in production form. It performs scene detection, keyframe extraction, transcript alignment, pacing analysis, energy-point detection, and content segmentation. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Turn one dense asset into analyzable fragments" ``` ## 4. `$SERVICE_NATURAL_CENTER` This service computes and stores the identity core. It should enforce symbolic, tonal, aesthetic, and thematic invariants across all outputs. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Compute, version, validate, and evolve the Natural Center object" ``` ## 5. `$SERVICE_GENERATION_ORCHESTRATOR` This service creates the outputs. It composes platform-specific assets, caption variants, CTA variants, sequencing plans, and campaign bundles. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Generate platform-native outputs constrained by identity and platform profile" ``` ## 6. `$SERVICE_PLATFORM_INTELLIGENCE` This is now mandatory. It models each platform as a time-dependent system. It should ingest official platform documentation changes, API changes, available analytics fields, posting constraints, and internal performance shifts. YouTube’s analytics surfaces already include audience retention, traffic and audience segments, while TikTok and LinkedIn expose developer posting capabilities and versioning or access constraints through official docs; those should be treated as structured inputs to this service. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Track platform state, detect change, update generation and scheduling policy" ``` ## 7. `$SERVICE_SCHEDULING_ENGINE` This service determines when each output should be published, republished, remixed, or retired. It consumes audience timing, asset decay models, and platform profile data. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Temporal orchestration" ``` ## 8. `$SERVICE_PUBLISHING_CONNECTORS` This is the execution layer. It handles the actual post creation and retrieval integrations. TikTok’s Content Posting APIs support direct posting or draft upload flows; LinkedIn’s Posts API supports creation and retrieval of organic and sponsored posts, with content-type constraints and versioned API requirements. ([developers.tiktok.com](https://developers.tiktok.com/products/content-posting-api?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Platform-specific publish / fetch adapters" ``` ## 9. `$SERVICE_ANALYTICS_INGESTION` This service pulls analytics and normalizes them into a common schema. YouTube official analytics surfaces support view, engagement, retention, CTR, and audience segmentation at varying levels, though some data may be limited depending on thresholds and filters. That limitation itself must be modeled as a reliability constraint. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Collect raw performance telemetry and normalize it" ``` ## 10. `$SERVICE_ATTRIBUTION_ENGINE` This is where the product stops being “content software” and becomes an economic system. It traces every post, fragment, caption, CTA, campaign, and asset back to origin. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Asset-level and fragment-level yield accounting" ``` ## 11. `$SERVICE_EXPERIMENTATION_ENGINE` This service owns A/B tests, bandits, holdouts, control/treatment assignment, confidence intervals, and causal inference workflow. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Statistically disciplined optimization" ``` ## 12. `$SERVICE_RESEARCH_REGISTRY` This is the bridge between dissertation-grade and operator-grade. Every hypothesis, paper mapping, experiment, metric definition, and platform-state memo should live here. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Research memory and epistemic governance" ``` ## 13. `$SERVICE_REPORTING_DASHBOARD` This serves operators, clients, and future investors. It must expose asset yield, audience movement, platform differences, and trend drift. ```bash $RESPONSIBILITY = "Human-readable operational truth" ``` # III. Canonical data domains You need four data planes, not one. ```bash $DATA_PLANES = { source_plane, operational_plane, experimental_plane, evolutionary_plane } ``` ## `$SOURCE_PLANE` This contains hero assets, renders, transcripts, briefs, brand corpus, prior posts, style references. ## `$OPERATIONAL_PLANE` This contains generated posts, publish events, platform IDs, schedules, revisions, connector logs. ## `$EXPERIMENTAL_PLANE` This contains hypotheses, treatments, controls, randomization groups, test windows, significance results. ## `$EVOLUTIONARY_PLANE` This contains platform snapshots, platform doc changes, field additions/removals, policy updates, performance regime shifts, and internal adaptation rules. # IV. Core database objects The system should be object-oriented at the ontology level and event-driven at the execution level. ```yaml Asset: asset_id: string source_uri: string asset_type: video|image_set|render_set|document brand_id: string checksum: string created_at: datetime Fragment: fragment_id: string asset_id: string fragment_type: clip|frame|crop|layout start_ms: int end_ms: int energy_score: float semantic_cluster: string NaturalCenter: nc_id: string brand_id: string thematic_core: json tonal_vector: json aesthetic_signature: json symbolic_markers: json invariants: json mutation_bounds: json version: int PlatformProfile: profile_id: string platform: youtube|instagram|tiktok|linkedin effective_from: date effective_to: date|null format_weights: json known_constraints: json ranking_assumptions: json confidence_score: float GeneratedUnit: unit_id: string fragment_id: string platform: string unit_type: short_video|carousel|image_post|text_post caption_variant_id: string cta_variant_id: string nc_version: int platform_profile_id: string PublishEvent: publish_id: string unit_id: string platform: string external_post_id: string published_at: datetime schedule_policy_id: string PerformanceObservation: obs_id: string publish_id: string observed_at: datetime metrics: json source_reliability: float Experiment: experiment_id: string hypothesis_id: string treatment_policy: json control_policy: json start_at: datetime end_at: datetime status: draft|running|complete PlatformChangeEvent: change_id: string platform: string detected_at: datetime change_type: api|analytics_field|format|policy|behavioral_shift evidence_uri: string effect_hypothesis: string severity: low|medium|high ``` # V. Platform evolution as a first-class subsystem This is the part most teams omit. It should not be “notes in a document.” It should be a live intelligence loop. ```bash $SUBSYSTEM_PLATFORM_EVOLUTION = { documentation_monitor, analytics_schema_monitor, posting_capability_monitor, performance_regime_monitor, historical_phase_model } ``` ## `$DOCUMENTATION_MONITOR` This tracks official docs, release notes, and API version changes. LinkedIn’s official docs already show versioned API behavior and deprecation notices, which is exactly the type of change this monitor must capture. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/linkedin/marketing/community-management/shares/posts-api?view=li-lms-2026-01&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$ANALYTICS_SCHEMA_MONITOR` This tracks whether a platform adds, removes, or changes analytics surfaces. On YouTube, limited-data conditions must be tracked because they alter what inference is valid. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$POSTING_CAPABILITY_MONITOR` This tracks what formats and workflows are currently supported. TikTok’s direct-post versus draft-upload modes and LinkedIn’s content-type support are examples. ([developers.tiktok.com](https://developers.tiktok.com/products/content-posting-api?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$PERFORMANCE_REGIME_MONITOR` This is internal. It detects when yesterday’s winning structures stop winning. Example: same asset class, same audience band, same cadence, but retention or follow-conversion drops across a time window. ## `$HISTORICAL_PHASE_MODEL` This stores platform eras. That lets you encode your point directly: ```yaml PlatformEra: platform: youtube era_id: YT_2010_CREATOR_GRAPH start_date: 2008-01-01 end_date: 2012-12-31 dominant_mechanism: "subscriber/personality pull" success_features: ["personality continuity", "episode loyalty", "creator identity"] ``` Then later: ```yaml PlatformEra: platform: youtube era_id: YT_2020_RETENTION_RECOMMENDER start_date: 2019-01-01 end_date: null dominant_mechanism: "recommendation + retention + multi-format coexistence" success_features: ["hook quality", "retention curve", "packaging", "viewer segmentation"] ``` The exact dates should remain provisional research objects unless directly established by your source methodology. The point is structural: **platform-state(t)** must exist as a model variable. # VI. The research operating system inside production The system must preserve dissertation-grade rigor while running. ```bash $RESEARCH_OS = { hypothesis_registry, source_registry, experiment_registry, evidence_store, conclusion_registry } ``` ## `$HYPOTHESIS_REGISTRY` Every important claim gets an ID. ```yaml Hypothesis: hypothesis_id: HYP_000127 title: "Open-loop short-form sequencing increases return-viewer ratio on platform X" pillar: behavioral_response platform_scope: ["youtube","tiktok"] temporal_scope: "current" status: proposed|running|supported|rejected ``` ## `$SOURCE_REGISTRY` This stores both secondary and primary sources. Secondary sources support theory. Primary sources support market and platform standing. For platform standing, official platform docs and analytics documentation qualify as primary sources for platform capabilities and measurement surfaces. ([support.google.com](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/9002587?hl=en&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ## `$EXPERIMENT_REGISTRY` This records every test and its outcome. ## `$EVIDENCE_STORE` This stores screenshots, exports, The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.

Make sure to include fileciteturn5file2 in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link. Correct sequencing is: ```text dissertation-grade → operator-grade ``` Reason: ```text Operator systems without deep grounding → brittle, overfit, non-transferable Dissertation layer → defines invariants that operator layer executes against ``` So we proceed with: ```bash $PHASE = "DISERTATION_GRADE_RESEARCH_ATLAS_V1" ``` --- # I. ATLAS STRUCTURE ```bash $RESEARCH_ATLAS = { pillars: [...], per_pillar: { canonical_sources, core_concepts, temporal_analysis, system_mapping, research_questions } } ``` --- # II. PILLAR 1 — CAUSAL INFERENCE ## Canonical Sources ```text - Judea Pearl — Causality (2009) - Hernán & Robins — Causal Inference (2020) - Imbens & Rubin — Causal Inference for Statistics (2015) - Angrist & Pischke — Mostly Harmless Econometrics (2008) ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - counterfactuals - treatment vs control - confounding variables - causal graphs (DAGs) ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Econometrics + advertising experiments ### Present Gap Content systems lack causal isolation ### Future (System) ```text fragment-level causal attribution ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "Attribution Engine" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text Which content features causally drive follower conversion? ``` --- # III. PILLAR 2 — INFORMATION THEORY ## Canonical Sources ```text - Claude Shannon — Mathematical Theory of Communication (1948) - Cover & Thomas — Elements of Information Theory (2006) - MacKay — Information Theory, Inference, and Learning (2003) ``` --- ## Core Concepts ```text - entropy - signal vs noise - compression - channel capacity ``` --- ## Temporal ### Past Communication systems optimization ### Present Gap Content treated as aesthetic, not signal ### Future ```text asset → maximum signal extraction → multi-channel transmission ``` --- ## Mapping ```bash $SYSTEM_COMPONENT = "Decomposition Engine" ``` --- ## Research Questions ```text What is the maximum extractable signal per asset? ``` --- # IV. STAGE 1 — SIGNAL EXTRACTION ## $PROC_SIGNAL_EXTRACTION ### Goal Extract **non-obvious recurring patterns**. --- ## Step 1.1 — Visual Pattern Extraction ```bash id="5aquu3" $PROC_VISUAL_PARSE ``` Outputs: ```yaml id="23z2gk" visual_features: color_distribution motion_profile composition_patterns subject_focus pacing_signature ``` --- ## Step 1.2 — Audio / Language Extraction (if present) ```bash id="tpifda" $PROC_LANGUAGE_PARSE ``` Outputs: ```yaml id="62vdab" linguistic_features: sentence_length_distribution lexical density tone polarity rhetorical structures ``` --- ## Step 1.3 — Semantic Compression ```bash id="1uszxl" $PROC_SEMANTIC_CLUSTER ``` Method: - embed frames / text - cluster embeddings - extract dominant themes Output: ```yaml id="1uyoim" themes: - cluster_1 - cluster_2 - cluster_3 ``` --- # IV. STAGE 2 — CENTER DERIVATION ## $PROC_CENTER_SYNTHESIS ### Goal Collapse extracted signals into a **low-entropy core**. --- ## Step 2.1 — Thematic Core ```bash id="4qd3bf" $THEMATIC_CORE = top_k(themes, k=3–5) ``` Constraint: - must be stable across assets - must be compressible to minimal expression --- ## Step 2.2 — Aesthetic Signature ```bash id="us6jb5" $AESTHETIC_SIGNATURE = normalize( color + composition + motion ) ``` Example structure: ```yaml id="xygfqk" aesthetic_signature: palette: [dark_neutrals, metallic_accents] motion: [slow, deliberate] framing: [center-weighted, negative-space heavy] ``` --- ## Step 2.3 — Tonal Vector ```bash id="q207nq" $TONAL_VECTOR = embed(language_features) ``` Axes: - formal ↔ casual - minimal ↔ expressive - cold ↔ emotional --- ## Step 2.4 — Narrative Bias ```bash id="c64z8h" $NARRATIVE_BIAS = detect_patterns( transformation, tension, reveal, authority, aspiration ) ``` --- ## Step 2.5 — Symbolic Markers ```bash id="6dnyrs" $SYMBOLIC_MARKERS = extract_recurring_elements ``` Examples: - visual motifs - phrases - structural beats --- ## Step 2.6 — Negative Space (CRITICAL) ```bash id="qpx92j" $NEGATIVE_SPACE = detect_absences ``` This defines: ```text id="r5kuv4" what the system must NEVER produce ``` Example: - no humor - no fast cuts - no bright palettes --- # V. Tonal Invariants ```yaml id="zcuv9u" rules: - "no slang" - "sentence length > threshold" ``` --- ### 3. To prevent this, he **swallowed each child at birth**. ### Children involved The children he consumed were: - Hestia - Demeter - Hera - Hades - Poseidon His wife, **Rhea**, eventually intervened: - She hid their youngest child, **Zeus** - She gave Cronus a **stone wrapped in cloth**, which he swallowed instead ### Resolution When Zeus matured, he forced Cronus to **regurgitate his siblings**, and then led the Olympians in overthrowing him (the Titanomachy). ### Structural interpretation (useful for your system work) Cronus represents: - **Self-consuming systems** (systems that destroy their own outputs to preserve control) - **Fear-driven recursion failure** - **Temporal devouring** (time consuming its own productions — later philosophically mapped onto Saturn/Time) If you want, I can map Cronus into your myth→technology assignment framework (e.g., which system archetype or computational failure mode he encodes).

ALG_01 — multimodal asset normalization Purpose: convert heterogeneous input into a unified analyzable representation. ### Accepted input classes Your sources already imply these classes: ```text video image_set / render_set audio text / brand voice corpus historical post corpus ``` fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file2 ### Canonical normalized object ```json { "asset_id": "A_*", "asset_type": "video|image_set|audio|document|mixed", "streams": { "video": [...], "audio": [...], "text": [...], "image": [...] }, "metadata": { "duration_ms": 0, "fps": 0, "resolution": [0, 0], "language": "en", "brand_id": "B_*" } } ``` ### Exact procedure ```text PROC_NORMALIZE(asset): 1. Compute checksum(asset) 2. Detect asset_type 3. If video: demux into video stream + audio stream transcode to canonical formats sample frames at adaptive interval 4. If image_set/render_set: normalize dimensions, color profile, EXIF 5. If audio: normalize sample rate and loudness 6. If text: clean, segment, and encode paragraphs/sentences 7. Persist all derivative streams 8. Emit asset.normalized ``` --- ## 2. ALG_02 — multimodal fragment extraction Purpose: produce candidate units from dense assets. Your sources already call for clip extraction at high-energy moments, scene detection, keyframe extraction, pacing analysis, and segmentation. fileciteturn2file0 fileciteturn2file1 ### 2.1 Video fragment extraction For video, define fragments over a change-point lattice rather than fixed windows. ```text Let V = video timeline Let C = set of candidate cut points C = union( scene boundaries, shot boundaries, transcript punctuation peaks, silence boundaries, audio onset peaks, motion regime changes ) ``` Construct candidate intervals: ```text F_video = {(c_i, c_j) | min_len <= c_j - c_i <= max_len} ``` Recommended bounds: ```text min_len = 3s max_len = 45s platform-specific downstream pruning applies ``` ### 2.2 Image fragment extraction For renders and stills, fragments are not temporal; they are compositional derivatives. ```text F_image = union( full-frame, crop_subject, crop_detail, before_after_pair, comparison_layout, lifestyle_context_projection, carousel_sequence ) ``` ### 2.3 Audio fragment extraction ```text F_audio = union( speech segment, hook phrase, emphasis burst, music drop, silence-contrast interval ) ``` ### 2.4 Text fragment extraction ```text F_text = union( claim, hook line, authority line, transformation line, CTA line, FAQ line ) ``` ### 2.5 Exact multimodal candidate generation ```text PROC_EXTRACT_FRAGMENTS(asset): 1. Detect scene boundaries on video 2. Detect shot boundaries 3. ASR transcript with token timestamps 4. Compute audio envelope, spectral flux, silence regions 5. Compute motion magnitude and saliency over time 6. Merge all boundary proposals 7. Generate candidate windows 8. Generate image crops/layout variants 9. Generate text units from transcript and corpus 10. Return multimodal candidate set F ``` --- ## 3. ALG_03 — Natural Center derivation This is already partially specified in the source corpus: thematic core, aesthetic signature, tonal vector, narrative bias, symbolic markers, negative space, invariants, mutation bounds. fileciteturn2file2 ### Exact Natural Center object ```json { "nc_id": "NC_*", "thematic_core": [t1, t2, t3, t4, t5], "aesthetic_signature": { "palette": [...], "motion": [...], "composition": [...], "texture": [...] }, "tonal_vector": [formal, expressive, emotional, authoritative, playful], "narrative_bias": [transformation, tension, reveal, aspiration, proof], "symbolic_markers": [...], "negative_space": [...], "invariants": [...], "mutation_bounds": {...}, "embedding": [...] } ``` ### Exact derivation algorithm ```text PROC_DERIVE_NATURAL_CENTER(corpus): 1. For all visual assets: extract color histograms, embeddings, composition maps, motion stats 2. For all text/audio assets: extract sentence-length distribution, lexical density, rhetoric markers, semantic embeddings 3. Cluster semantic embeddings -> candidate themes 4. Select stable top-k themes across corpus 5. Compute aesthetic centroid across visual features 6. Compute tonal centroid across language features 7. Detect recurring symbolic elements 8. Detect systematic absences -> negative_space 9. Generate invariants from high-frequency stable features 10. Set mutation bounds from historical variance 11. Encode final Natural Center embedding ``` ### Stability rule A feature enters the Natural Center only if: ```text stability(feature) = presence_across_assets * consistency_across_assets accept iff stability(feature) >= τ_stability ``` --- ## 4. ALG_04 — fragment scoring This is the core extraction-scoring layer you asked for. Each candidate fragment gets a multimodal score vector, then a scalar aggregate. ### 4.1 Base feature vector per fragment For each fragment `f`, compute: ```text x(f) = [ visual_saliency, motion_energy, cut_density, subject_prominence, face_presence, product_presence, text_overlay_legibility, audio_energy, speech_clarity, hook_density, semantic_novelty, thematic_alignment, tonal_alignment, narrative_tension, symbolic_marker_density, platform_shape_fit ] ``` ### 4.2 Exact sub-scores #### Visual saliency ```text visual_saliency(f) = mean_t saliency_map_intensity(t) over fragment ``` #### Motion energy ```text motion_energy(f) = mean_t ||optical_flow(t)|| normalized by asset baseline ``` #### Cut density ```text cut_density(f) = number_of_shot_changes / fragment_duration_sec ``` #### Subject prominence ```text subject_prominence(f) = mean_t (largest_subject_bbox_area / frame_area) ``` #### Product presence ```text product_presence(f) = visible_time_of_primary_object / fragment_duration ``` #### Audio energy ```text audio_energy(f) = RMS(audio_signal_f) z-scored within asset ``` #### Speech clarity ```text speech_clarity(f) = 1 - WER_proxy(f) ``` Use ASR confidence as a proxy if needed. #### Hook density Define hooks as high-information lines or events in the first N seconds. ```text hook_density(f) = (count of hook tokens + count of high-saliency events in first 3s) / 3 ``` #### Semantic novelty ```text semantic_novelty(f) = distance(embedding(f), centroid(previous_selected_fragments)) ``` This prevents near-duplicate output. #### Thematic alignment ```text thematic_alignment(f) = max cosine_similarity(embedding(f), thematic_core_i) ``` #### Tonal alignment ```text tonal_alignment(f) = 1 - normalized_distance(tone_vector(f), tone_vector(NC)) ``` #### Narrative tension ```text narrative_tension(f) = weighted_sum( unresolved_question_presence, contrast_event_density, reveal_probability, expectation_violation ) ``` #### Symbolic marker density ```text symbolic_marker_density(f) = matched_symbolic_markers / possible_symbolic_markers ``` #### Platform shape fit This is structural, before any performance data. ```text platform_shape_fit(f,p) = g(duration_fit, aspect_ratio_fit, opening_velocity_fit, subtitle_density_fit) ``` ### 4.3 Hard constraint gating Before aggregate scoring, fail fragments that violate invariant rules: ```text if violates_negative_space(f, NC): reject if tonal_alignment(f) < τ_tone: reject if text_legibility(f,p) < τ_legibility: reject if safe_crop_confidence(f,p) < τ_crop: reject ``` ### 4.4 Aggregate fragment score ```text fragment_score(f,p) = w1*visual_saliency + w2*motion_energy + w3*subject_prominence + w4*audio_energy + w5*hook_density + w6*thematic_alignment + w7*tonal_alignment + w8*narrative_tension + w9*symbolic_marker_density + w10*semantic_novelty + w11*platform_shape_fit - w12*redundancy_penalty - w13*constraint_risk ``` Where: ```text redundancy_penalty = max similarity to already-selected fragments constraint_risk = predicted probability of violating NC or platform constraints ``` ### 4.5 Selection rule ```text select top-N fragments per platform subject to: pairwise similarity <= τ_dup thematic coverage >= τ_coverage duration bucket diversity narrative role diversity ``` This is better than naive top-N. --- ## 5. ALG_05 — platform-fit scoring Your sources explicitly require platform-aware generation and platform profiles with ranking features, format weights, timing bias, and time-varying platform state. fileciteturn2file1 ### Platform profile ```json { "platform": "tiktok", "format_weights": { "short_video": 0.8, "carousel": 0.1, "text": 0.1 }, "ranking_features": ["hook_retention", "replayability", "comment_rate"], "timing_bias": {...}, "constraints": {...}, "confidence_score": 0.82 } ``` ### Exact platform-fit score ```text platform_fit(f,p,t) = a1*duration_fit(f,p) + a2*opening_pattern_fit(f,p) + a3*caption_density_fit(f,p) + a4*loopability(f,p) + a5*replayability(f,p) + a6*format_weight_match(f,p,t) + a7*current_platform_preference_match(p,t) ``` ### Platform-specific heuristics ```text YouTube Shorts: emphasize clarity + retention + pay-off density TikTok: emphasize opening disruption + replayability + comment provocation LinkedIn: emphasize authority framing + slower pacing + insight density `` The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.

The core document defines the MVP as: one premium asset in, many platform-optimized posts out, then scheduled publishing plus weekly reporting on reach, engagement, and leads. fileciteturn2file0 The later system documents correctly extend that into a research-grade operating system with explicit `assets`, `fragments`, `generated_units`, `publish_events`, and `performance_observations`, and they already sketch the attribution path as `performance → publish_event → unit → fragment → asset`. fileciteturn2file1 The more advanced operator document also makes clear that attribution must not stop at “engagement”; it must connect output to audience movement, economics, and platform state over time. fileciteturn2file2 ## Source-grounded conclusion The mathematically correct attribution model for this system is not single-touch and not purely last-post. It has to be a **hierarchical, time-decayed, multi-touch attribution graph** with five levels: ```text Asset → Fragment → GeneratedUnit → PublishEvent → PerformanceObservation → AudienceStateTransition / EconomicOutcome ``` That is the only structure consistent with the source documents’ architecture, metrics, and stated goal of turning content generation into an economic engine rather than a posting tool. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Formal attribution model Let: ```text a ∈ A # source asset f ∈ F(a) # fragment derived from asset a u ∈ U(f) # generated unit derived from fragment f p ∈ P(u) # publish event for unit u o ∈ O(p) # observation window for publish event p k ∈ K # metric type t # time ``` Define base observed metrics per publish event and time window: ```text y_{p,k,t} ``` Examples: ```text k ∈ {views, watch_time, engagement, followers_gained, leads, revenue} ``` The source material already implies these as the primary and derived metrics: engagement, followers, leads, asset yield, follow conversion, retention ratio, and ROI. fileciteturn2file0turn2file1turn2file2 ## Structural lineage weights Every publish event inherits from an upstream chain. Define deterministic lineage indicators: ```text L_asset(a,p) = 1 if publish event p descends from asset a else 0 L_fragment(f,p) = 1 if p descends from fragment f else 0 L_unit(u,p) = 1 if p is generated from unit u else 0 ``` If a post is composed from multiple fragments or mixed inputs, replace binary lineage with normalized weights: ```text w_{a→p} ≥ 0, Σ_a w_{a→p} = 1 w_{f→p} ≥ 0, Σ_f w_{f→p} = 1 w_{u→p} ≥ 0, Σ_u w_{u→p} = 1 ``` This is necessary because a carousel, remix, or stitched cut can carry more than one source fragment. ## Time-decayed metric accumulation A publish event continues to generate value after publication. Let the contribution of publish event `p` to metric `k` over its full life be: ```text M_{p,k} = ∫_0^T y_{p,k}(τ) · d_k(τ) dτ ``` where `d_k(τ)` is a decay kernel. A practical discrete version: ```text M_{p,k} = Σ_{t=0}^{T} y_{p,k,t} · exp(-λ_k t) ``` where: ```text λ_k > 0 ``` Different metrics should use different decay rates. For example, engagement can decay faster than leads; follower gains may decay slower than likes but faster than attributed revenue. This is aligned with the source demand to model content half-life rather than only first-spike engagement. fileciteturn2file1turn2file2 ## Platform-normalized value Because platform conditions differ, raw metrics must be normalized by platform and time regime. Let: ```text z_{p,k} = (M_{p,k} - μ_{platform,format,k,era}) / σ_{platform,format,k,era} ``` Then define a platform-adjusted scalar value for a publish event: ```text V_p = Σ_k α_k · z_{p,k} ``` where `α_k` are strategic weights. Example: ```text α_watch_time > α_likes α_followers_gained > α_comments α_leads > α_followers when commercial objective dominates ``` This is required because the system documents explicitly treat platform state as time-varying and platform-specific. fileciteturn2file2 ## Publish-event attribution to upstream objects Now attribute publish-event value upward. ### Unit attribution ```text Attr(u) = Σ_{p ∈ P(u)} w_{u→p} · V_p ``` ### Fragment attribution ```text Attr(f) = Σ_{p} w_{f→p} · V_p ``` ### Asset attribution ```text Attr(a) = Σ_{p} w_{a→p} · V_p ``` This is the direct generalization of the source’s prototype query: ```sql SELECT asset_id, SUM(engagement) FROM performance_observations JOIN publish_events USING (publish_id) JOIN generated_units USING (unit_id) GROUP BY asset_id; ``` but generalized to weighted, multi-metric, time-decayed, platform-normalized attribution rather than naive summed engagement. fileciteturn2file1 ## Audience-state-transition attribution The more advanced documents redefine the system around audience formation, not just content output. They specify the audience chain: ```text Viewer → Engaged Viewer → Follower → Returning Follower → Converted User → Advocate ``` fileciteturn2file2 So define audience states: ```text S = {0,1,2,3,4,5,6} ``` with transitions: ```text 0→1 unaware→viewer 1→2 viewer→engaged 2→3 engaged→follower 3→4 follower→returning 4→5 returning→converted 5→6 converted→advocate ``` For each publish event `p`, estimate transition counts: ```text T_{p,i→j} ``` Then define transition value weights: ```text β_{i→j} ``` and a state-transition value: ```text Q_p = Σ_{i→j} β_{i→j} · T_{p,i→j} ``` Then total publish-event value becomes: ```text V_p* = V_p + Q_p ``` This lets attribution model not only post performance, but the actual audience movement the sources identify as the system’s core purpose. fileciteturn2file2 ## Economic attribution Let the cost basis for asset `a` be: ```text C_a = asset_production_cost + processing_cost + publishing_cost + overhead_allocated ``` Let the asset-attributed economic returns be: ```text R_a = γ_1 · Attr_followers(a) + γ_2 · Attr_leads(a) + γ_3 · Attr_revenue(a) + γ_4 · Attr_brand_value(a) ``` Then: ```text GrossAssetReturn(a) = R_a NetAssetReturn(a) = R_a - C_a ROI(a) = (R_a - C_a) / C_a AssetYieldMultiplier(a) = R_a / C_a ``` This directly matches the source ambition to move from “beautiful video” to measurable yield, leads, and ROI. fileciteturn2file0turn2file2 ## Causal rather than correlational attribution The source set explicitly says the system must move from correlation to causal inference and treat every post as a micro-experiment. fileciteturn1file0turn2file1 So observational attribution above is necessary but insufficient. Add a causal lift term. For a treatment variant `r` relative to control `c`, define: ```text Lift_{k} = E[Y_k | do(r)] - E[Y_k | do(c)] ``` In randomized deployment: ```text \hat{Lift}_{k} = mean(Y_k | treatment) - mean(Y_k | control) ``` Then fragment-level or unit-level causal value is: ```text CausalAttr(u,k) = n_u · \hat{Lift}_{u,k} ``` where `n_u` is treatment exposure count. For non-randomized deployments, use a doubly robust estimate: ```text DR_k = 1/n Σ_i [ m_1(x_i) - m_0(x_i) + z_i (y_i - m_1(x_i))/e(x_i) - (1-z_i)(y_i - m_0(x_i))/(1-e(x_i)) ] ``` where: ```text z_i = treatment indicator e(x_i) = propensity score m_1, m_0 = outcome models ``` Then use `DR_k` as the causal increment rather than raw observed delta. ## Natural-center constraint in attribution The source set repeatedly insists that every output inherit from a Natural Center and that identity be measurable and enforceable. fileciteturn2file2 So attribution should penalize off-brand wins that damage long-term system coherence. Let: ```text NCScore_p ∈ [0,1] ``` Then define identity-adjusted event value: ```text V_p^NC = V_p* · (1 - ρ + ρ·NCScore_p) ``` where `ρ ∈ [0,1]` controls how strongly identity coherence matters. If `NCScore_p < θ_min`, either reject the unit entirely or discount its attributed value sharply: ```text V_p^NC = 0 if NCScore_p < θ_min_reject ``` This prevents the The file is too long and its contents have been truncated.

The source set already supports the move from a single-operator product into a full multi-tenant SaaS. The core product thesis is stable: one premium asset becomes many platform-native outputs with measurable downstream yield. fileciteturn0file15 The operator-grade documents then extend that into a system with Natural Center enforcement, attribution, experimentation, platform intelligence, and event-driven workflows. fileciteturn0file1turn0file0 The correct expansion is therefore: ```text single-brand content engine → multi-tenant SaaS control plane → orchestration-backed execution plane → research / attribution / adaptation plane ``` ## Canonical system identity ```bash $SYSTEM_NAME="cronus-metabolus" $PRODUCT_SURFACE="auto-content-cannibal" $SYSTEM_CLASS="multi-tenant temporal content yield and audience formation SaaS" $ARCHITECTURE_STYLE="control-plane + orchestration-plane + tenant-isolated data-plane" ``` ## What changes when this becomes SaaS The original product is effectively: ```text asset in → fragments → generated posts → scheduling → weekly report ``` fileciteturn0file15 The SaaS form must become: ```text tenant → workspace → brand profiles → assets → orchestration workflows → platform publishing → analytics ingestion → attribution → experiments → reports ``` That implies four new first-class capabilities. First, tenant isolation. Every object in the system must belong to a tenant and optionally to a sub-brand or workspace. Second, orchestration. Every long-running process must run through a durable workflow engine rather than synchronous application logic. The source blueprint already points to Temporal as the correct workflow engine. fileciteturn0file0turn0file1 Third, policy separation. System policy, tenant policy, brand policy, and platform policy must be independently versioned. Fourth, multi-surface control. One control plane for operators and clients; one execution plane for workflows and workers. ## SaaS macro-architecture The clean form is this: ```text [ Web App / API Gateway ] | v [ SaaS Control Plane ] | v [ Orchestration Layer ] | v [ Domain Services ] | v [ Tenant-Isolated Data + Object Storage + Analytics ] ``` ### 1. Control plane This is where humans interact with the system. It should own authentication, billing context, tenant management, workspace configuration, brand setup, approvals, campaign setup, and report access. Minimal applications: ```text operator-console tenant-admin-console reporting-dashboard gateway-api ``` This is a direct expansion of the repo skeleton already implied in the sources. fileciteturn0file13 ### 2. Orchestration layer This is the core addition you asked for. The orchestration layer should not be a thin queue wrapper. It should be the durable stateful execution fabric for all long-lived processes: ```text asset intake workflow media analysis workflow natural center derivation workflow generation workflow approval workflow publishing workflow analytics backfill workflow attribution recompute workflow experiment workflow platform-intelligence refresh workflow ``` The source implementation blueprint already frames the system as event-driven with a workflow engine and an explicit asset pipeline. fileciteturn0file0turn0file1 Recommended identity: ```bash $WORKFLOW_ENGINE="Temporal" $ORCHESTRATION_ROLE="durable execution, retries, compensation, human approval gates, replay" ``` ### 3. Domain services These remain largely the same as in the operator-grade sources, but now they run as tenant-aware services behind orchestration. Canonical services: ```text auth-identity tenant-management billing-metering asset-ingestion media-analysis natural-center generation-orchestrator platform-intelligence scheduling-engine publishing-connectors analytics-ingestion attribution-engine experimentation-engine research-registry notification-service audit-log-service policy-engine ``` The core service map is already source-grounded; tenant-management, billing-metering, notification-service, and audit-log-service are the necessary SaaS completions. fileciteturn0file1turn0file13 ## Multi-tenant domain model The original documents define `Asset`, `Fragment`, `NaturalCenter`, `GeneratedUnit`, `PublishEvent`, `PerformanceObservation`, `Experiment`, and `PlatformProfile`. fileciteturn0file1turn0file14 For SaaS, prepend tenant structure above them: ```text Tenant → Workspace → BrandProfile → NaturalCenter → Asset → Fragment → GeneratedUnit → PublishEvent → PerformanceObservation → Experiment → Report ``` ### Canonical tenancy hierarchy ```yaml Tenant: tenant_id: string name: string plan_id: string status: active|suspended|trial created_at: datetime Workspace: workspace_id: string tenant_id: string name: string region: string created_at: datetime BrandProfile: brand_id: string tenant_id: string workspace_id: string name: string industry: string platform_policy_id: string default_natural_center_id: string TenantUser: user_id: string tenant_id: string workspace_id: string role: owner|admin|editor|analyst|viewer BrandConnection: connection_id: string tenant_id: string brand_id: string platform: youtube|tiktok|linkedin|instagram credential_ref: string status: active|expired|revoked ``` Then all downstream objects must include at minimum: ```text tenant_id workspace_id brand_id ``` This is non-negotiable if the system is to support white-label agencies, enterprise clients, and direct brands simultaneously, which the product thesis explicitly anticipates. fileciteturn0file15 ## Orchestration layer design This layer should model workflows as explicit state machines with tenant-scoped execution context. ### Execution contract Every workflow should carry: ```yaml WorkflowContext: tenant_id: string workspace_id: string brand_id: string actor_id: string correlation_id: string workflow_id: string policy_snapshot_id: string platform_profile_version: string natural_center_version: string ``` ### Core workflow families #### Asset pipeline workflow ```text asset.uploaded → asset.normalized → fragments.extracted → natural_center.loaded_or_derived → candidates.scored → units.generated → approval.requested → publish.plan.created ``` #### Publish workflow ```text publish.plan.created → platform.connector.validated → post.rendered → post.published → publish.confirmed → analytics.poll.scheduled ``` #### Analytics and attribution workflow ```text analytics.poll.started → analytics.raw.ingested → metrics.normalized → attribution.recomputed → audience-transition.updated → report.materialized ``` #### Experiment workflow ```text experiment.created → treatment.assignment.generated → units.variantized → publish.routed → metrics.collected → significance.computed → strategy.updated ``` #### Platform intelligence workflow ```text platform-doc.refresh → schema.diff.detected → capability.delta.detected → platform-profile.revised → tenant-policies.revalidated → alert.emitted ``` These are direct continuations of the workflow and platform-intelligence logic already present in the source set. fileciteturn0file1turn0file0 ## Isolation model A real multi-tenant SaaS needs explicit isolation doctrine. ### Data isolation Recommended default: ```text shared application cluster + shared control-plane services + row-level isolation in operational DB + tenant-scoped object-store prefixes + tenant-scoped warehouse partitions ``` For enterprise plans, add optional logical or physical isolation: ```text dedicated database schema or dedicated deployment cell per tenant ``` ### Storage pattern ```bash $OBJECT_STORE_URI $OBJECT_STORE_BUCKET_RAW $OBJECT_STORE_BUCKET_DERIVED $OBJECT_STORE_BUCKET_REPORTS $TENANT_OBJECT_PREFIX="tenants/$TENANT_ID/" ``` Example pathing: ```text tenants/T_001/brands/B_044/assets/A_938/raw/hero-film.mp4 tenants/T_001/brands/B_044/fragments/F_193/clip_01.mp4 tenants/T_001/brands/B_044/reports/R_2026_03_weekly.pdf ``` ### Security model Every request path must resolve authorization through: ```text user → tenant membership → workspace membership → role → brand permission → action policy ``` And every workflow execution must be auditable. ##
